500	
501	# ✅ Arithmetic with $((...))
502	z_result=$((z_value * 2 + 10))    # Instead of: expr or bc
503	```
504	
505	### Temp Files Instead of Command Substitution
506	
507	All external command output capture uses temp files. The three prohibited `$()` patterns are: `local z_var=$(cmd)` (local swallows exit status), pipelines inside `$()` (hidden intermediate failures), and unguarded `$()` (silent failure). The only `$()` permitted on functions is for `_capture` (secrets that must not touch disk) and `_recite` (read-only roll access) — both with explicit `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`. Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) is also permitted in `$()` as no file-based alternative exists.
508	
509	```bash
510	# ❌ Anti-pattern: nested command substitution
511	z_result=$(echo $(complex_command) | process)  # Hidden failures!
512	
513	# ❌ Anti-pattern: unguarded command substitution
514	z_timestamp=$(date +%s)  # Failure is silent!
515	
516	# ✅ Correct: temp files make failures visible afterward
517	complex_command > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
518	process < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
519	read -r z_result < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "No result"
520	
521	# ✅ Correct: single external command via temp file
522	date +%s > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Failed to get timestamp"
523	local -r z_timestamp=$(<"${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}")
524	test -n "${z_timestamp}" || buc_die "Empty timestamp"
525	
526	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: Secrets use _capture functions, never temp files
527	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: _recite functions return via stdout (read-only roll access)
528	```
529	
530	**Temp file lifecycle**: Temp files under `BURD_TEMP_DIR` are **preserved after execution** for forensic debugging. Never delete temp files in module code — their persistence is intentional. Cleanup is handled by infrastructure outside BCG's scope.
531	
532	### Stderr Capture — Never Suppress
533	
534	Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.
535	
536	```bash
537	# ❌ Anti-pattern: stderr suppressed — failure cause invisible
538	curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>/dev/null \
539	  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url}"
540	
541	# ✅ Correct: stderr captured to temp file, referenced in die message
542	curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
543	  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
544	```
545	
546	**Stderr file naming**: Use a kindle `_PREFIX` constant (grouped with other temp file prefixes for collision visibility) and append a discriminator at the usage site.
547	
548	**In loops**, use an auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness rather than trusting loop data values to be unique:
549	
550	```bash
551	# In kindle — grouped with sibling temp file prefixes
552	Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_"
553	
554	# In command function — integer discriminator ensures uniqueness
555	local z_index=0
556	while IFS='|' read -r z_varname z_image z_tag || test -n "${z_varname}"; do
557	  local z_manifest_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
558	  local z_stderr_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_inspect_stderr.txt"
559	
560	  docker manifest inspect "${z_image}:${z_tag}" \
561	    > "${z_manifest_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
562	    || buc_die "Failed to inspect ${z_image}:${z_tag} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
563	
564	  z_index=$((z_index + 1))
565	done
566	```
567	
568	### String Usage
569	
570	```bash
571	# Single-line HERE-string allowed for piping variables
572	jq '.field' <<<"${z_json_data}"
573	grep "pattern" <<<"${z_content}"
574	
575	# Multi-line HERE-strings and HERE-docs prohibited
576	```
577	
578	### Readonly Patterns
579	
580	Readonly enforcement makes mutation bugs loud at the point of violation rather than silent cascades downstream. Three patterns at different scopes:
581	
582	#### `local -r` — Default for Local Variables
583	
584	Use `local -r` for every local variable that is assigned once and never modified. This is the majority of locals in a typical BCG function.
585	
586	```bash
587	# ✅ Default: readonly locals
588	local -r z_filepath="${1:-}"
589	local -r z_varname="${z_buv_varname_roll[$z_idx]}"
590	local -r z_full_ref="${z_image}@${z_digest}"
591	local -r z_content=$(<"${z_file}")    # $(<file) is a builtin — safe on one line
592	```
593	
594	**Four exceptions** where `local` (mutable) is required:
595	
596	```bash
597	# Exception 1: Loop counters
598	local z_index=0
599	z_index=$((z_index + 1))
600	
601	# Exception 2: Per-iteration synthesized variables (reassigned each pass)
602	local z_manifest_file=""
603	for z_spec in "${z_specs[@]}"; do
604	  z_manifest_file="${Z_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
605	  ...
606	done
607	
608	# Exception 3: Two-line capture pattern (readonly would lock empty value)
609	local z_token
610	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
611	
612	# Exception 4: Accumulators and state-tracking flags
613	local z_any_failed=0
614	local z_current_group=""
615	```
616	
617	**Why not `local -i`?** `local -i` silently coerces non-integers to 0 — it hides bugs. `local -r` does the opposite — it surfaces mutation bugs loudly. Same flag syntax, opposite safety properties.
618	
619	#### `readonly` — Every Kindle Constant
620	
621	Every variable assigned in a kindle function is a constant. Apply `readonly` to every assignment — whether the value is a literal, computed from other variables, or composed from prior assignments:
622	
623	```bash
624	z«prefix»_kindle() {
625	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
626	
627	  # Simple kindle constants (literal values, but defined in kindle for readonly enforcement)
628	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_API_VERSION="v2"
629	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_MAX_RETRIES=3
630	
631	  # Computed from already-set values
632	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_REGISTRY_HOST="${RBGD_LOCATION}${RBGC_HOST_SUFFIX}"
633	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"
634	
635	  # Composed from prior kindle assignments
636	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_ACCEPT_TYPES="${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_A},${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_B}"
637	
638	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
639	}
640	```
641	
642	If a kindle function uses intermediate variables to build up a result (e.g., composing array elements), the final assignment is still `readonly`. Intermediates that are truly local to the build-up should use `local -r`.
643	
644	**The KINDLED sentinel** is a kindle constant — it gets `readonly` like every other kindle assignment. The `test -z` guard at the top of kindle fires before any reassignment attempt, so `readonly` is belt-and-suspenders: the guard gives a friendly error message, `readonly` is the backstop.
645	
646	#### Mutable Kindle State
647	
648	Some kindle functions initialize mutable state alongside constants — counters, accumulators, registry rolls, and builder state that are modified after kindle returns. These variables **must not** be `readonly`, but they must be visually distinguishable from kindle constants.
649	
650	**Convention**: Mutable kindle state uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name`. Constants use **UPPERCASE** `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`. Case alone signals mutability.
651	
652	```bash
653	z«prefix»_kindle() {
654	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
655	
656	  # ✅ Kindle constants — UPPERCASE, readonly
657	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"
658	
659	  # ✅ Mutable kindle state — lowercase, NO readonly
660	  z_«prefix»_file_index=0              # counter incremented post-kindle
661	  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()              # registry populated by enroll calls
662	  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""           # builder state set during enrollment
663	
664	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
665	}
666	```
667	
668	**Rules**:
669	- Mutable kindle state follows the same `z_` internal prefix as roll arrays
670	- Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state
671	- If a variable is initialized in kindle and never mutated afterward, it is a constant — use `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`
672	- The `_roll` suffix is conventional for parallel-array registries but not required for all mutable state (counters, flags, builder state use descriptive names)
673	
674	**Test reset**: When tests need fresh enrollment state, provide an internal reset function that clears mutable kindle state without touching `KINDLED`. Re-kindling a module is never correct — the sentinel is `readonly`.
675	
676	```bash
677	# ✅ Test support: reset mutable state, module stays kindled
678	z«prefix»_reset_enrollment() {
679	  z«prefix»_sentinel
680	  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()
681	  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""
682	  # ... clear all mutable kindle state ...
683	}
684	```
685	
686	**The re-source trap** — `readonly` belongs in code, never in sourceable `.env` files:
687	```bash
688	# ❌ NEVER in sourceable .env files
689	# If a .env file contains `readonly FOO=bar`, sourcing it a second time
690	# (e.g., regime reload, test re-initialization) dies with:
691	#   bash: FOO: readonly variable
692	```
693	
694	#### `readonly VAR` — Lock After Enforce
695	
696	For regime variables loaded from `.env` files, lock them *after* validation succeeds. This is the regime archetype's lock step (see Regime Module Archetype above):
697	
698	```bash
699	z«prefix»_enforce() {
700	  z«prefix»_sentinel
701	  buv_vet «SCOPE»
702	  # ... custom format checks ...
703	}
704	
705	# In the lock step (called after enforce succeeds):
706	# Lock all enrolled variables — any downstream mutation is now a loud error
707	readonly REGI_FIELD_ONE REGI_FIELD_TWO REGI_FIELD_THREE
708	
709	# Derived state built from validated values:
710	Z«PREFIX»_DOCKER_ENV=("-e" "REGI_FIELD_ONE=${REGI_FIELD_ONE}")
711	```
712	
713	**Ordering matters**: Build derived state (docker env arrays, rollup strings) *after* enforce but *before* or *during* the lock call. Once variables are readonly, derived state captures validated, immutable values.
714	
715	## Special Function Definitions
716	
717	### Special Function Patterns
718	
719	This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.
720	
721	| Type      | Pattern                        | First Line           | Returns                      | buc_step                  | Contract                            |
722	|-----------|--------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------|
723	| Predicate | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | 0=true, 1=false              | No (use buc_log_«source») | Never dies, status only             |
724	| Capture   | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_capture`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout once at end or exit 1 | No (use buc_log_«source») | Clean error handling, single return |
725	| Enroll    | `[z]«prefix»_[«scope»_]enroll` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `z_«funcname»_«retval»` vars | No (use buc_log_«source») | Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) in kindle only; returns via variables |
726	| Recite    | `[z]«prefix»_«what»_recite`    | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout or exit 1             | No (use buc_log_«source») | Read-only access to rolls; never mutates |
727	| Fixture baste | `z«tb»_«name»_baste`         | —                   | — (side effects only)        | Optional                  | Fixture subshell; kindle, source, configure |
728	| Tcase     | `«tc»_«name»_tcase`           | —                    | — (exit status to suite)     | No                        | Case subshell; assertions, verification |
729	| Yawp      | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_yawp`     | `z«module»_sentinel` | `z_«module»_«group»` var    | No                        | Pure assignment, cannot fail, no output |
730	
731	---
732	
733	#### Capture Functions (controlled command substitution)
734	
735	**Purpose: Return a single processed value through stdout with clean error handling.**
736	
737	Capture functions encapsulate command substitution to ensure predictable error handling and single-point-of-return semantics.
738	
739	```bash
740	z«prefix»_«name»_capture() {
741	  z«prefix»_sentinel
742	
743	  # Process and validate internally
744	  local z_result
745	  z_result=$(some_command | process) || return 1
746	
747	  # Validate without buc_die
748	  test -n "${z_result}" || return 1
749	
750	  # Single output at end
751	  echo "${z_result}"
752	}
753	
754	# Caller uses two-line pattern
755	local z_value
756	z_value=$(z«prefix»_«name»_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture value"
757	```
758	
759	**Use capture functions for:**
760	- Secrets that must never touch disk
761	- Values requiring command substitution or pipeline processing
762	- Any computed value where clean error handling matters
763	
764	**Contract:**
765	- Returns stdout once at end or exits with status 1
766	- Never uses `buc_die` internally
767	- No side effects beyond the returned value
768	- May source credential files when necessary for security
769	- May use `buc_log_«source»` for forensic trail (writes to transcript file only)
770	- Never writes secrets to disk
771	- Never writes to stderr
772	
773	#### Predicate Functions (status only, no output)
774	
775	Predicate functions are used to make decisions, not indicating error.
776	
777	```bash
778	z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate() {
779	  z«prefix»_sentinel
780	
781	  local z_file="${1:-}"
782	
783	  # Return status only - no output, no dies
784	  test -f "${z_file}" || return 1
785	  grep -q "required_pattern" "${z_file}" || return 1
786	
787	  return 0
788	}
789	
790	# Caller uses in conditionals
791	z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate "${z_config}" || buc_warn "Config invalid, using defaults"
792	```
793	
794	#### Enroll Functions (kindle-only registry population)
795	
796	**Purpose**: Populate parallel-array registries (rolls) during module initialization. Enroll functions solve the subshell problem: when a function both mutates shared state and returns a value, calling it in `$()` loses the mutations.
797	
798	**The parallel-array registry pattern (rolls)**:
799	- Kindle initializes N empty arrays (rolls) of the same length
800	- Enroll validates inputs and appends atomically to all rolls
801	- Recite functions (see below) provide read-only access after kindle completes
802	- Variable naming: `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll` for all roll arrays (internal state, initialized in kindle)
803	
804	**Example** (using synthetic notation — «reg» owns the rolls, «con» consumes them):
805	
806	```bash
807	# Module «reg» — owns the rolls
808	z«reg»_kindle() {
809	  test -z "${Z«REG»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"
810	
811	  z_«reg»_name_roll=()
812	  z_«reg»_target_roll=()
813	  z_«reg»_handler_roll=()
814	
815	  Z«REG»_KINDLED=1   # MUST be last — sentinel guards all subsequent calls
816	}
817	
818	# Enroll function — called by consuming module's kindle, NOT «reg»'s own kindle
819	«reg»_enroll() {
820	  z«reg»_sentinel   # Safe: «reg» is already kindled when consumer calls this
821	
822	  local z_name="${1:-}"
823	  local z_target="${2:-}"
824	  local z_handler="${3:-}"
825	  test -n "${z_name}" || buc_die "enroll: name required"
826	
827	  # Registration-time validation
828	  declare -F "${z_handler}" >/dev/null || buc_die "enroll: handler not found: ${z_handler}"
829	
830	  z_«reg»_name_roll+=("${z_name}")
831	  z_«reg»_target_roll+=("${z_target}")
832	  z_«reg»_handler_roll+=("${z_handler}")
833	
834	  # Return via variable — NOT echo
835	  z_«reg»_enroll_name="${z_name}"
836	}
837	
838	# Module «con» — consumes the registry
839	z«con»_kindle() {
840	  test -z "${Z«CON»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"
841	
842	  # «reg» must be kindled first (rolls initialized, sentinel passes)
843	  «reg»_enroll "alpha" "/path/alpha" "handle_alpha"
844	  Z«CON»_DEFAULT="${z_«reg»_enroll_name}"
845	
846	  «reg»_enroll "bravo" "/path/bravo" "handle_bravo"
847	
848	  Z«CON»_KINDLED=1   # MUST be last
849	}
850	```
851	
852	**Key constraints**:
853	- Enroll functions may ONLY be called within kindle
854	- Rolls are initialized in kindle, populated by enroll in kindle, then immutable after kindle completes
855	- Enroll functions work exclusively with rolls (parallel arrays) — no other shared state mutation
856	- Registration-time validation: check invariants at enroll time, not at recite/execution time
857	- Return values use `z_«funcname»_«retval»` convention (function name embedded verbatim for traceability)
858	- Must NOT be called inside `$()` — side effects would be lost
859	- May use `buc_die` internally (unlike `_capture`)
860	
861	**Two-level registries**: When entities have parent-child relationships (e.g., groups containing items), use two flat registries with a foreign-key column rather than per-parent dynamic arrays. This avoids `eval` and stays bash 3.2 safe.
862	
863	**Contract summary**:
864	- Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) only
865	- Returns value(s) via `z_«funcname»_«retval»` variables (NOT echo)
866	- Called exclusively within kindle
867	- Should rarely be used — only when a function must both mutate and return
868	
869	#### Recite Functions (read-only roll access)
870	
871	**Purpose: Provide read-only access to roll arrays populated by enroll functions.**
872	
873	Recite functions are the only access path to rolls after kindle completes. They must never mutate roll arrays.
874	
875	```bash
876	«prefix»_«what»_recite() {
877	  z«prefix»_sentinel
878	
879	  local z_key="${1:-}"
880	  test -n "${z_key}" || return 1
881	
882	  local z_i=0
883	  for z_i in "${!z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
884	    test "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[$z_i]}" = "${z_key}" || continue
885	    echo "${z_«prefix»_target_roll[$z_i]}" || return 1
886	    return 0
887	  done
888	  return 1
889	}
890	
891	# Caller uses two-line capture pattern
892	local z_target
893	z_target=$(«prefix»_target_recite "alpha") || buc_die "not found"
894	```
895	
896	**Contract:**
897	- Read-only: MUST NOT mutate roll arrays or any shared state
898	- Returns via echo (like capture functions) — safe to call in `$()`
899	- Returns 1 on not-found (like `_capture`) — never uses `buc_die` internally
900	- Caller decides error handling: `|| buc_die` or conditional
901	- Usable anywhere after kindle completes (not restricted to kindle)
902	
903	#### Yawp Functions (pure-computation group return)
904	
905	**Purpose: Produce a value into a shared group return variable via pure bash assignment. No subshells, no stdout, no stderr.**
906	
907	Named for Whitman's "barbaric yawp" — a loud, unmistakeable declaration into a known place. Yawp functions solve the high-frequency value-production problem: when dozens of calls per function each need a computed string, `$()` subshell overhead is disproportionate to the work being done.
908	
909	```bash
910	# «prefix»_«name»_yawp arg1 [arg2] — what it stamps
911	# → local -r z_name="${z_«module»_«group»}"
912	«prefix»_«name»_yawp() {
913	  z«module»_sentinel
914	  z_«module»_«group»="${ZMOD_PREFIX}${1:-}${ZMOD_SUFFIX}"
915	}
916	```
917	
918	The two-line comment is the per-function template: signature on line 1, mandatory capture pattern on line 2. The `→` arrow is a visual reminder that the caller must capture on the same line.
919	
920	**Return variable**: `z_«module»_«group»` — mutable kindle state, initialized in kindle. One per function group. All `_yawp` functions in a group share the same return variable. The value is valid until the next `_yawp` call in the same group.
921	
922	```bash
923	# In kindle — initialize mutable kindle state for yawp group
924	z_«module»_«group»=""
925	
926	# In function — pure assignment, no process spawning
927	«prefix»_cmd_yawp() {
928	  z«module»_sentinel
929	  z_«module»_«group»="${ZMOD_MARKER_CMD}${1:-}${ZMOD_MARKER_END}"
930	}
931	
932	# Caller pattern — semicolon capture on same line
933	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status";  local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
934	«prefix»_ui_yawp "Save";         local -r z_ui="${z_«module»_«group»}"
935	```
936	
937	**Contract:**
938	- Sets `z_«module»_«group»` (mutable kindle state, initialized in kindle)
939	- **Caller MUST capture to `local -r` on the same line** — semicolon-separated, value is destroyed by the next `_yawp` call in the same group
940	- Pure bash builtins only — no external commands, no process spawning
941	- Cannot fail — no `|| buc_die` needed at call site
942	- Never uses `buc_die` internally
943	- No stdout, no stderr
944	- First line: sentinel check
945	
946	**Capture discipline — the return variable is a transient slot, not a durable value:**
947	
948	```bash
949	# CORRECT — semicolon capture on same line, compose from captured locals
950	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status";  local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
951	«prefix»_tt_yawp "rbw-rrv";      local -r z_tt="${z_«module»_«group»}"
952	buh_line "Run ${z_cmd} to validate: ${z_tt}"
953	
954	# AVOID — two-line form works but doesn't enforce discipline
955	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
956	local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
957	# ↑ Nothing prevents inserting code between yawp and capture.
958	# The semicolon form makes interleaving structurally impossible.
959	
960	# WRONG — z_«group» used directly in output (fragile, unreadable)
961	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
962	buh_line "Run ${z_«module»_«group»}"
963	
964	# WRONG — two yawps before capture (first value lost)
965	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
966	«prefix»_tt_yawp "rbw-rrv"
967	buh_line "${z_«module»_«group»}"  # only has tt, cmd is gone
968	```
969	
970	The semicolon makes the capture discipline visually self-enforcing — you cannot accidentally insert code between the yawp and its capture. The two-line form invited interleaving mistakes; the one-line form eliminates them structurally.
971	
972	The one-yawp-one-capture rhythm is the invariant. Pre-captured constants (e.g., `RBYC_*` from `zrbyc_kindle`) are safe to interpolate directly because they were captured at kindle time — the discipline applies at the yawp call site, not at the interpolation site.
973	
974	**Use yawp functions for:**
975	- Pure string assembly (concatenation, marker stamping)
976	- Value production where `$()` subshell overhead is unnecessary
977	- High-frequency call sites (dozens of calls per function)
978	- Any computed value that uses only bash builtins and cannot fail
979	
980	**Do NOT use yawp functions for:**
981	- Functions that can fail → use `_capture`
982	- Functions with side effects beyond the return variable → use regular functions
983	- Functions that mutate rolls → use `_enroll`
984	- Functions that call external commands → use `_capture` or temp files
985	
986	---
987	
988	## When to Use Special Functions
989	
990	- **_capture**: Need a value that requires command substitution or clean error handling
991	- **_predicate**: Need true/false for conditional logic without dying
992	- **_enroll**: Populate parallel-array registries (rolls) during kindle with validated inputs and return values
993	- **_recite**: Read-only access to roll arrays populated by enroll functions
994	- **_baste**: Fixture preparation inside fixture subshell (kindle, source, configure)
995	- **_tcase**: Test case verification function inside `_tcase` subshell
996	- **_yawp**: Pure-computation value production via group return variable. High-frequency call sites where `$()` overhead is disproportionate. String assembly, marker stamping, path construction.
997	- **Neither**: Use temp files for multi-step pipelines, direct `|| buc_die` for simple failures
998	
999	## Naming Convention Patterns

file.content:
# ✅ Arithmetic with $((...))
z_result=$((z_value * 2 + 10))    # Instead of: expr or bc
```

### Temp Files Instead of Command Substitution

All external command output capture uses temp files. The three prohibited `$()` patterns are: `local z_var=$(cmd)` (local swallows exit status), pipelines inside `$()` (hidden intermediate failures), and unguarded `$()` (silent failure). The only `$()` permitted on functions is for `_capture` (secrets that must not touch disk) and `_recite` (read-only roll access) — both with explicit `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`. Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) is also permitted in `$()` as no file-based alternative exists.

```bash
# ❌ Anti-pattern: nested command substitution
z_result=$(echo $(complex_command) | process)  # Hidden failures!

# ❌ Anti-pattern: unguarded command substitution
z_timestamp=$(date +%s)  # Failure is silent!

# ✅ Correct: temp files make failures visible afterward
complex_command > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
process < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
read -r z_result < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "No result"

# ✅ Correct: single external command via temp file
date +%s > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Failed to get timestamp"
local -r z_timestamp=$(<"${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}")
test -n "${z_timestamp}" || buc_die "Empty timestamp"

# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: Secrets use _capture functions, never temp files
# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: _recite functions return via stdout (read-only roll access)
```

**Temp file lifecycle**: Temp files under `BURD_TEMP_DIR` are **preserved after execution** for forensic debugging. Never delete temp files in module code — their persistence is intentional. Cleanup is handled by infrastructure outside BCG's scope.

### Stderr Capture — Never Suppress

Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.

```bash
# ❌ Anti-pattern: stderr suppressed — failure cause invisible
curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>/dev/null \
  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url}"

# ✅ Correct: stderr captured to temp file, referenced in die message
curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
```

**Stderr file naming**: Use a kindle `_PREFIX` constant (grouped with other temp file prefixes for collision visibility) and append a discriminator at the usage site.

**In loops**, use an auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness rather than trusting loop data values to be unique:

```bash
# In kindle — grouped with sibling temp file prefixes
Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_"

# In command function — integer discriminator ensures uniqueness
local z_index=0
while IFS='|' read -r z_varname z_image z_tag || test -n "${z_varname}"; do
  local z_manifest_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
  local z_stderr_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_inspect_stderr.txt"

  docker manifest inspect "${z_image}:${z_tag}" \
    > "${z_manifest_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to inspect ${z_image}:${z_tag} — see ${z_stderr_file}"

  z_index=$((z_index + 1))
done
```

### String Usage

```bash
# Single-line HERE-string allowed for piping variables
jq '.field' <<<"${z_json_data}"
grep "pattern" <<<"${z_content}"

# Multi-line HERE-strings and HERE-docs prohibited
```

### Readonly Patterns

Readonly enforcement makes mutation bugs loud at the point of violation rather than silent cascades downstream. Three patterns at different scopes:

#### `local -r` — Default for Local Variables

Use `local -r` for every local variable that is assigned once and never modified. This is the majority of locals in a typical BCG function.

```bash
# ✅ Default: readonly locals
local -r z_filepath="${1:-}"
local -r z_varname="${z_buv_varname_roll[$z_idx]}"
local -r z_full_ref="${z_image}@${z_digest}"
local -r z_content=$(<"${z_file}")    # $(<file) is a builtin — safe on one line
```

**Four exceptions** where `local` (mutable) is required:

```bash
# Exception 1: Loop counters
local z_index=0
z_index=$((z_index + 1))

# Exception 2: Per-iteration synthesized variables (reassigned each pass)
local z_manifest_file=""
for z_spec in "${z_specs[@]}"; do
  z_manifest_file="${Z_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
  ...
done

# Exception 3: Two-line capture pattern (readonly would lock empty value)
local z_token
z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"

# Exception 4: Accumulators and state-tracking flags
local z_any_failed=0
local z_current_group=""
```

**Why not `local -i`?** `local -i` silently coerces non-integers to 0 — it hides bugs. `local -r` does the opposite — it surfaces mutation bugs loudly. Same flag syntax, opposite safety properties.

#### `readonly` — Every Kindle Constant

Every variable assigned in a kindle function is a constant. Apply `readonly` to every assignment — whether the value is a literal, computed from other variables, or composed from prior assignments:

```bash
z«prefix»_kindle() {
  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"

  # Simple kindle constants (literal values, but defined in kindle for readonly enforcement)
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_API_VERSION="v2"
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_MAX_RETRIES=3

  # Computed from already-set values
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_REGISTRY_HOST="${RBGD_LOCATION}${RBGC_HOST_SUFFIX}"
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"

  # Composed from prior kindle assignments
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_ACCEPT_TYPES="${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_A},${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_B}"

  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
}
```

If a kindle function uses intermediate variables to build up a result (e.g., composing array elements), the final assignment is still `readonly`. Intermediates that are truly local to the build-up should use `local -r`.

**The KINDLED sentinel** is a kindle constant — it gets `readonly` like every other kindle assignment. The `test -z` guard at the top of kindle fires before any reassignment attempt, so `readonly` is belt-and-suspenders: the guard gives a friendly error message, `readonly` is the backstop.

#### Mutable Kindle State

Some kindle functions initialize mutable state alongside constants — counters, accumulators, registry rolls, and builder state that are modified after kindle returns. These variables **must not** be `readonly`, but they must be visually distinguishable from kindle constants.

**Convention**: Mutable kindle state uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name`. Constants use **UPPERCASE** `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`. Case alone signals mutability.

```bash
z«prefix»_kindle() {
  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"

  # ✅ Kindle constants — UPPERCASE, readonly
  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"

  # ✅ Mutable kindle state — lowercase, NO readonly
  z_«prefix»_file_index=0              # counter incremented post-kindle
  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()              # registry populated by enroll calls
  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""           # builder state set during enrollment

  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
}
```

**Rules**:
- Mutable kindle state follows the same `z_` internal prefix as roll arrays
- Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state
- If a variable is initialized in kindle and never mutated afterward, it is a constant — use `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`
- The `_roll` suffix is conventional for parallel-array registries but not required for all mutable state (counters, flags, builder state use descriptive names)

**Test reset**: When tests need fresh enrollment state, provide an internal reset function that clears mutable kindle state without touching `KINDLED`. Re-kindling a module is never correct — the sentinel is `readonly`.

```bash
# ✅ Test support: reset mutable state, module stays kindled
z«prefix»_reset_enrollment() {
  z«prefix»_sentinel
  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()
  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""
  # ... clear all mutable kindle state ...
}
```

**The re-source trap** — `readonly` belongs in code, never in sourceable `.env` files:
```bash
# ❌ NEVER in sourceable .env files
# If a .env file contains `readonly FOO=bar`, sourcing it a second time
# (e.g., regime reload, test re-initialization) dies with:
#   bash: FOO: readonly variable
```

#### `readonly VAR` — Lock After Enforce

For regime variables loaded from `.env` files, lock them *after* validation succeeds. This is the regime archetype's lock step (see Regime Module Archetype above):

```bash
z«prefix»_enforce() {
  z«prefix»_sentinel
  buv_vet «SCOPE»
  # ... custom format checks ...
}

# In the lock step (called after enforce succeeds):
# Lock all enrolled variables — any downstream mutation is now a loud error
readonly REGI_FIELD_ONE REGI_FIELD_TWO REGI_FIELD_THREE

# Derived state built from validated values:
Z«PREFIX»_DOCKER_ENV=("-e" "REGI_FIELD_ONE=${REGI_FIELD_ONE}")
```

**Ordering matters**: Build derived state (docker env arrays, rollup strings) *after* enforce but *before* or *during* the lock call. Once variables are readonly, derived state captures validated, immutable values.

## Special Function Definitions

### Special Function Patterns

This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.

| Type      | Pattern                        | First Line           | Returns                      | buc_step                  | Contract                            |
|-----------|--------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Predicate | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | 0=true, 1=false              | No (use buc_log_«source») | Never dies, status only             |
| Capture   | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_capture`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout once at end or exit 1 | No (use buc_log_«source») | Clean error handling, single return |
| Enroll    | `[z]«prefix»_[«scope»_]enroll` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `z_«funcname»_«retval»` vars | No (use buc_log_«source») | Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) in kindle only; returns via variables |
| Recite    | `[z]«prefix»_«what»_recite`    | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout or exit 1             | No (use buc_log_«source») | Read-only access to rolls; never mutates |
| Fixture baste | `z«tb»_«name»_baste`         | —                   | — (side effects only)        | Optional                  | Fixture subshell; kindle, source, configure |
| Tcase     | `«tc»_«name»_tcase`           | —                    | — (exit status to suite)     | No                        | Case subshell; assertions, verification |
| Yawp      | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_yawp`     | `z«module»_sentinel` | `z_«module»_«group»` var    | No                        | Pure assignment, cannot fail, no output |

---

#### Capture Functions (controlled command substitution)

**Purpose: Return a single processed value through stdout with clean error handling.**

Capture functions encapsulate command substitution to ensure predictable error handling and single-point-of-return semantics.

```bash
z«prefix»_«name»_capture() {
  z«prefix»_sentinel

  # Process and validate internally
  local z_result
  z_result=$(some_command | process) || return 1

  # Validate without buc_die
  test -n "${z_result}" || return 1

  # Single output at end
  echo "${z_result}"
}

# Caller uses two-line pattern
local z_value
z_value=$(z«prefix»_«name»_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture value"
```

**Use capture functions for:**
- Secrets that must never touch disk
- Values requiring command substitution or pipeline processing
- Any computed value where clean error handling matters

**Contract:**
- Returns stdout once at end or exits with status 1
- Never uses `buc_die` internally
- No side effects beyond the returned value
- May source credential files when necessary for security
- May use `buc_log_«source»` for forensic trail (writes to transcript file only)
- Never writes secrets to disk
- Never writes to stderr

#### Predicate Functions (status only, no output)

Predicate functions are used to make decisions, not indicating error.

```bash
z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate() {
  z«prefix»_sentinel

  local z_file="${1:-}"

  # Return status only - no output, no dies
  test -f "${z_file}" || return 1
  grep -q "required_pattern" "${z_file}" || return 1

  return 0
}

# Caller uses in conditionals
z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate "${z_config}" || buc_warn "Config invalid, using defaults"
```

#### Enroll Functions (kindle-only registry population)

**Purpose**: Populate parallel-array registries (rolls) during module initialization. Enroll functions solve the subshell problem: when a function both mutates shared state and returns a value, calling it in `$()` loses the mutations.

**The parallel-array registry pattern (rolls)**:
- Kindle initializes N empty arrays (rolls) of the same length
- Enroll validates inputs and appends atomically to all rolls
- Recite functions (see below) provide read-only access after kindle completes
- Variable naming: `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll` for all roll arrays (internal state, initialized in kindle)

**Example** (using synthetic notation — «reg» owns the rolls, «con» consumes them):

```bash
# Module «reg» — owns the rolls
z«reg»_kindle() {
  test -z "${Z«REG»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"

  z_«reg»_name_roll=()
  z_«reg»_target_roll=()
  z_«reg»_handler_roll=()

  Z«REG»_KINDLED=1   # MUST be last — sentinel guards all subsequent calls
}

# Enroll function — called by consuming module's kindle, NOT «reg»'s own kindle
«reg»_enroll() {
  z«reg»_sentinel   # Safe: «reg» is already kindled when consumer calls this

  local z_name="${1:-}"
  local z_target="${2:-}"
  local z_handler="${3:-}"
  test -n "${z_name}" || buc_die "enroll: name required"

  # Registration-time validation
  declare -F "${z_handler}" >/dev/null || buc_die "enroll: handler not found: ${z_handler}"

  z_«reg»_name_roll+=("${z_name}")
  z_«reg»_target_roll+=("${z_target}")
  z_«reg»_handler_roll+=("${z_handler}")

  # Return via variable — NOT echo
  z_«reg»_enroll_name="${z_name}"
}

# Module «con» — consumes the registry
z«con»_kindle() {
  test -z "${Z«CON»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"

  # «reg» must be kindled first (rolls initialized, sentinel passes)
  «reg»_enroll "alpha" "/path/alpha" "handle_alpha"
  Z«CON»_DEFAULT="${z_«reg»_enroll_name}"

  «reg»_enroll "bravo" "/path/bravo" "handle_bravo"

  Z«CON»_KINDLED=1   # MUST be last
}
```

**Key constraints**:
- Enroll functions may ONLY be called within kindle
- Rolls are initialized in kindle, populated by enroll in kindle, then immutable after kindle completes
- Enroll functions work exclusively with rolls (parallel arrays) — no other shared state mutation
- Registration-time validation: check invariants at enroll time, not at recite/execution time
- Return values use `z_«funcname»_«retval»` convention (function name embedded verbatim for traceability)
- Must NOT be called inside `$()` — side effects would be lost
- May use `buc_die` internally (unlike `_capture`)

**Two-level registries**: When entities have parent-child relationships (e.g., groups containing items), use two flat registries with a foreign-key column rather than per-parent dynamic arrays. This avoids `eval` and stays bash 3.2 safe.

**Contract summary**:
- Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) only
- Returns value(s) via `z_«funcname»_«retval»` variables (NOT echo)
- Called exclusively within kindle
- Should rarely be used — only when a function must both mutate and return

#### Recite Functions (read-only roll access)

**Purpose: Provide read-only access to roll arrays populated by enroll functions.**

Recite functions are the only access path to rolls after kindle completes. They must never mutate roll arrays.

```bash
«prefix»_«what»_recite() {
  z«prefix»_sentinel

  local z_key="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_key}" || return 1

  local z_i=0
  for z_i in "${!z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
    test "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[$z_i]}" = "${z_key}" || continue
    echo "${z_«prefix»_target_roll[$z_i]}" || return 1
    return 0
  done
  return 1
}

# Caller uses two-line capture pattern
local z_target
z_target=$(«prefix»_target_recite "alpha") || buc_die "not found"
```

**Contract:**
- Read-only: MUST NOT mutate roll arrays or any shared state
- Returns via echo (like capture functions) — safe to call in `$()`
- Returns 1 on not-found (like `_capture`) — never uses `buc_die` internally
- Caller decides error handling: `|| buc_die` or conditional
- Usable anywhere after kindle completes (not restricted to kindle)

#### Yawp Functions (pure-computation group return)

**Purpose: Produce a value into a shared group return variable via pure bash assignment. No subshells, no stdout, no stderr.**

Named for Whitman's "barbaric yawp" — a loud, unmistakeable declaration into a known place. Yawp functions solve the high-frequency value-production problem: when dozens of calls per function each need a computed string, `$()` subshell overhead is disproportionate to the work being done.

```bash
# «prefix»_«name»_yawp arg1 [arg2] — what it stamps
# → local -r z_name="${z_«module»_«group»}"
«prefix»_«name»_yawp() {
  z«module»_sentinel
  z_«module»_«group»="${ZMOD_PREFIX}${1:-}${ZMOD_SUFFIX}"
}
```

The two-line comment is the per-function template: signature on line 1, mandatory capture pattern on line 2. The `→` arrow is a visual reminder that the caller must capture on the same line.

**Return variable**: `z_«module»_«group»` — mutable kindle state, initialized in kindle. One per function group. All `_yawp` functions in a group share the same return variable. The value is valid until the next `_yawp` call in the same group.

```bash
# In kindle — initialize mutable kindle state for yawp group
z_«module»_«group»=""

# In function — pure assignment, no process spawning
«prefix»_cmd_yawp() {
  z«module»_sentinel
  z_«module»_«group»="${ZMOD_MARKER_CMD}${1:-}${ZMOD_MARKER_END}"
}

# Caller pattern — semicolon capture on same line
«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status";  local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
«prefix»_ui_yawp "Save";         local -r z_ui="${z_«module»_«group»}"
```

**Contract:**
- Sets `z_«module»_«group»` (mutable kindle state, initialized in kindle)
- **Caller MUST capture to `local -r` on the same line** — semicolon-separated, value is destroyed by the next `_yawp` call in the same group
- Pure bash builtins only — no external commands, no process spawning
- Cannot fail — no `|| buc_die` needed at call site
- Never uses `buc_die` internally
- No stdout, no stderr
- First line: sentinel check

**Capture discipline — the return variable is a transient slot, not a durable value:**

```bash
# CORRECT — semicolon capture on same line, compose from captured locals
«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status";  local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
«prefix»_tt_yawp "rbw-rrv";      local -r z_tt="${z_«module»_«group»}"
buh_line "Run ${z_cmd} to validate: ${z_tt}"

# AVOID — two-line form works but doesn't enforce discipline
«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
# ↑ Nothing prevents inserting code between yawp and capture.
# The semicolon form makes interleaving structurally impossible.

# WRONG — z_«group» used directly in output (fragile, unreadable)
«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
buh_line "Run ${z_«module»_«group»}"

# WRONG — two yawps before capture (first value lost)
«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
«prefix»_tt_yawp "rbw-rrv"
buh_line "${z_«module»_«group»}"  # only has tt, cmd is gone
```

The semicolon makes the capture discipline visually self-enforcing — you cannot accidentally insert code between the yawp and its capture. The two-line form invited interleaving mistakes; the one-line form eliminates them structurally.

The one-yawp-one-capture rhythm is the invariant. Pre-captured constants (e.g., `RBYC_*` from `zrbyc_kindle`) are safe to interpolate directly because they were captured at kindle time — the discipline applies at the yawp call site, not at the interpolation site.

**Use yawp functions for:**
- Pure string assembly (concatenation, marker stamping)
- Value production where `$()` subshell overhead is unnecessary
- High-frequency call sites (dozens of calls per function)
- Any computed value that uses only bash builtins and cannot fail

**Do NOT use yawp functions for:**
- Functions that can fail → use `_capture`
- Functions with side effects beyond the return variable → use regular functions
- Functions that mutate rolls → use `_enroll`
- Functions that call external commands → use `_capture` or temp files

---

## When to Use Special Functions

- **_capture**: Need a value that requires command substitution or clean error handling
- **_predicate**: Need true/false for conditional logic without dying
- **_enroll**: Populate parallel-array registries (rolls) during kindle with validated inputs and return values
- **_recite**: Read-only access to roll arrays populated by enroll functions
- **_baste**: Fixture preparation inside fixture subshell (kindle, source, configure)
- **_tcase**: Test case verification function inside `_tcase` subshell
- **_yawp**: Pure-computation value production via group return variable. High-frequency call sites where `$()` overhead is disproportionate. String assembly, marker stamping, path construction.
- **Neither**: Use temp files for multi-step pipelines, direct `|| buc_die` for simple failures

## Naming Convention Patterns

1025	| Test case file               | `«prefix»tc«xx»_«Name».sh`  | `rbtckk_KickTires.sh`        | N/A      | PascalCase name               |
1026	| Fixture baste function       | `z«tb»_«name»_baste`        | `zrbtb_ark_baste`            | Testbench| snake_case, fixture layer     |
1027	| Case function                | `«tc»_«name»_tcase`         | `rbtckk_false_tcase`         | Test case| snake_case, case layer        |
1028	| Yawp function (public)       | `«prefix»_«name»_yawp`      | `buyy_cmd_yawp`              | Impl     | snake_case                    |
1029	| Yawp function (internal)     | `z«prefix»_«name»_yawp`     | `zbuyf_resolve_yawp`         | Impl     | snake_case                    |
1030	| Yawp return variable         | `z_«module»_«group»`        | `z_buym_yelp`                | Impl     | snake_case, mutable kindle state |
1031	
1032	---
1033	
1034	## Runtime Visibility vs Comments
1035	
1036	### ✅ Use Runtime Visibility
1037	
1038	```bash
1039	# ✅ Use Runtime Visibility
1040	buc_step "Fetching manifest from registry"          # User milestones
1041	buc_log_args "Processing platform linux/amd64"      # Variable details
1042	jq '.response' file.json | buc_log_pipe            # Pipeline output
1043	
1044	# When to use each source:
1045	buc_log_args - Simple messages, variable values, progress markers
1046	buc_log_pipe - Command output, JSON responses, multi-line data
1047	```
1048	
1049	### ❌ Never Use Comments for Runtime Info
1050	
1051	```bash
1052	# Building JWT header         ❌ Wrong
1053	buc_log_args "Building JWT header"  ✅ Correct
1054	
1055	# Process each manifest       ❌ Wrong
1056	buc_step "Processing manifests" ✅ Correct
1057	```
1058	
1059	Comments are ONLY for code clarification that doesn't matter at runtime.
1060	
1061	---
1062	
1063	## Test vs Bracket Expressions
1064	
1065	### ✅ Use test Command instead of `[[ xxx ]]/fi` where possible
1066	
1067	```bash
1068	# Error, warning, and optional reporting
1069	test -f "${z_file}" || buc_die "File not found"
1070	test "${z_var}" = "expected" || buc_warn "Unexpected value"
1071	test -n "${z_param}" || buc_step "Running with zero length parameter"
1072	```
1073	
1074	### ✅ Exception: Pattern Matching Only
1075	
1076	```bash
1077	# Only acceptable use of [[ ]] - pattern matching requires it
1078	[[ "${z_filename}" =~ \.tar\.gz$ ]] && z_extension="tar.gz"
1079	```
1080	
1081	### ❌ Anti-Patterns
1082	
1083	```bash
1084	# Avoid [[ ]] for simple tests
1085	[[ -f "${z_file}" ]]           # Use: test -f "${z_file}"
1086	[[ "${z_var}" == "value" ]]    # Use: test "${z_var}" = "value"
1087	[[ -n "${z_param}" ]]          # Use: test -n "${z_param}"
1088	
1089	# Never use test with command substitution that might expand to empty
1090	# test with ZERO arguments returns TRUE (exit 0) — silent pass landmine
1091	test $(echo "${z_val}" | grep -E '^pattern$')  # ❌ Succeeds when grep matches nothing!
1092	
1093	# ✅ Use exit-status predicates instead
1094	echo "${z_val}" | grep -qE '^pattern$' || buc_die "Invalid format"
1095	
1096	# ✅ Or use case statement for pattern matching
1097	case "${z_val}" in
1098	  *pattern*) buc_log_args "Found pattern" ;;
1099	  *) buc_die "Pattern not found" ;;
1100	esac
1101	```
1102	
1103	**Note**: Use `=` not `==` in test expressions for POSIX compliance.
1104	
1105	### ❌ Subshell Exit vs Brace-Group
1106	
1107	```bash
1108	# ❌ exit in subshell kills only the subshell — script continues
1109	some_cmd || (echo "ERROR" >&2 && exit 1)
1110	
1111	# ✅ Brace-group stays in same process — exit/return works correctly
1112	some_cmd || { echo "ERROR" >&2; exit 1; }
1113	```
1114	
1115	**Rule**: Error blocks must use `{ ...; }` not `( ... )` when intending to exit or return. The `(subshell)` creates a new process — `exit` terminates only that subshell, not the calling script.
1116	
1117	### ✅ Legitimate Subshell Uses
1118	
1119	The prohibition above is specific to error-handling blocks where `exit`/`return` must reach the calling shell. Subshells are legitimate in these contexts:
1120	
1121	- **Test execution** — principled isolation between suites and cases; see **Test Execution Patterns**
1122	- **`$()` command substitution** — inside `_capture`/`_recite` functions only, or bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`); see **Capture Functions** and **Command Substitution Rules** checklist
1123	- **Isolation subshells** — environment containment with exit-status propagation (below)
1124	
1125	#### Isolation Subshells (environment containment)
1126	
1127	When a sequence of commands mutates the shell environment (sourcing files, `cd`, venv activation, `export`), a subshell prevents those mutations from leaking into subsequent commands. The exit status propagates across the `)` boundary, so the caller can detect and handle failures.
1128	
1129	**Rarely needed.** Most code operates within a single kindled environment. Use isolation subshells only when you must execute commands that change the environment and those changes must not persist.
1130	
1131	**Two variants** — same principle, different caller handling:
1132	
1133	```bash
1134	# Variant 1: Error propagation — die on any internal failure
1135	(
1136	  source "${z_config_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source: ${z_config_file}"
1137	  «prefix»_operation || buc_die "Operation failed"
1138	) || buc_die "Isolation subshell failed"
1139	
1140	# Variant 2: Status capture — caller inspects and decides
1141	local z_status=0
1142	(
1143	  source "${z_config_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source: ${z_config_file}"
1144	  «prefix»_operation || buc_die "Operation failed"
1145	) || z_status=$?
1146	```
1147	
1148	Inside the subshell, `buc_die` calls `exit` which terminates only the subshell — producing a non-zero exit status that the outer `|| buc_die` or `|| z_status=$?` catches. Failure propagates through exit status, not through `exit` reaching the parent.
1149	
1150	**Constraints:**
1151	- Every command inside must have explicit `|| buc_die` — `set -e` is suppressed inside `( ... ) ||` (as with all BCG code, never rely on it)
1152	- The outer boundary must have `|| buc_die` or `|| z_status=$?` — never leave the subshell exit status unchecked
1153	- Sourcing inside the subshell is permitted (this is a primary use case — isolation prevents sourced variables from leaking)
1154	- Keep subshell bodies short and focused — if the body grows complex, delegate to a CLI script via `exec`
1155	- `$( ... ) || buc_die` is legitimate when you need both isolation and output capture — `$()` is a subshell, so isolation holds and the two-line capture pattern applies
1156	
1157	**Use cases:**
1158	- Iterating config files that define overlapping variables (e.g., sourcing each nameplate `.env` in a loop)
1159	- Running commands that require `cd` to a different directory
1160	- Activating a virtual environment for a single operation
1161	- Any sequence where environment mutations must not outlive the block
1162	
1163	**Anti-pattern — no internal error handling:**
1164	
1165	```bash
1166	# ❌ Failures inside subshell are silent — source or echo can fail undetected
1167	(
1168	  source "${z_file}"
1169	  echo "${SOME_VAR}"
1170	) || buc_die "Subshell failed"
1171	
1172	# ✅ Every command explicitly handled
1173	(
1174	  source "${z_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source: ${z_file}"
1175	  echo "${SOME_VAR}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
1176	) || buc_die "Subshell failed"
1177	```
1178	
1179	---
1180	
1181	## Prohibited Constructs
1182	
1183	### ❌ HERE Documents (Heredocs)
1184	
1185	```bash
1186	# Never use - breaks indentation
1187	cat <<EOF > "${z_file}"
1188	content here
1189	must be unindented
1190	EOF
1191	
1192	# Use temp files or echo sequences instead
1193	echo "content here" > "${z_file}"
1194	echo "stays indented" >> "${z_file}"
1195	```
1196	
1197	### ❌ Bash 4+ Features
1198	
1199	```bash
1200	# Avoid these (bash 3.2 compatibility)
1201	declare -A z_array              # Associative arrays
1202	z_files=(**/*.txt)              # ** globbing
1203	readarray -t z_lines            # readarray/mapfile
1204	```
1205	
1206	### ❌ Bare File Truncation
1207	
1208	```bash
1209	# ❌ Looks like a line where the command was accidentally deleted
1210	> "${z_file}"
1211	
1212	# ✅ Explicit no-op makes truncation intent unambiguous
1213	: > "${z_file}"
1214	```
1215	
1216	Bare `> file` without a command is valid bash but reads like an accident at 3am. The `: >` form explicitly signals "I am creating or truncating this file." Shellcheck enforces this via SC2188.
1217	
1218	**Prefer immutable temp files.** BCG temp files are forensic artifacts — preserved for post-mortem debugging. If you find yourself truncating a temp file, the design likely needs a unique filename per step instead of reusing and clearing. Truncation is appropriate only for log files and other non-forensic outputs.
1219	
1220	### ❌ Inline Shellcheck Directives
1221	
1222	```bash
1223	# ❌ Never suppress shellcheck inline
1224	# shellcheck disable=SC2086
1225	some_command ${z_unquoted}
1226	
1227	# ❌ Never use source path hints
1228	# shellcheck source=/dev/null
1229	source "${z_file}"
1230	```
1231	
1232	All BCG-structural shellcheck suppressions live in `busc_shellcheckrc`. No inline `# shellcheck` directives of any kind. If a shellcheck code fires and it's BCG-structural, add it to `busc_shellcheckrc` with rationale. If it's a genuine finding, fix the code.
1233	
1234	### ❌ Commit-Message Comments
1235	
1236	A **commit-message comment** describes the *change event* rather than the *code*. It narrates what was added, when, or why it was inserted — information that belongs in `git log`, not in the file. The tell: the comment would make more sense as a commit summary than as documentation for a future reader.
1237	
1238	```bash
1239	# ❌ Commit-message comment: narrates the insertion
1240	# Added qualification subsystem for shellcheck support
1241	buw-qsc) exec "${z_buq_cli}" buq_shellcheck "$@" ;;
1242	
1243	# ❌ Commit-message comment: documents the change, not the code
1244	# New helper for retry logic (see PR #142)
1245	zrbf_retry_with_backoff() {
1246	
1247	# ✅ No comment needed — the code is self-evident
1248	buw-qsc) exec "${z_buq_cli}" buq_shellcheck "$@" ;;
1249	
1250	# ✅ Comment describes the code, not the change
1251	# Retry with exponential backoff; max delay capped at 60s
1252	zrbf_retry_with_backoff() {
1253	```
1254	
1255	**Litmus test**: Would this comment be better as a commit message? If yes, delete it from the code — it's already captured in git history.
1256	
1257	This is a characteristic LLM failure mode. Models narrate their own work process into code, writing for the person reviewing the diff rather than the person reading the file next year. The resulting comments assume a supervisory reader with conversation context — an audience that does not exist at maintenance time.
1258	
1259	---
1260	
1261	## Interface Contamination Discipline
1262	
1263	**Interface contamination** is any change that expands the accepted input space, invocation forms, or alternative paths through an interface without explicit specification requirement.
1264	
1265	### Zeroes Theory
1266	
1267	Every tolerance, alias, fallback, or alternative path multiplies the enumerated state space. The litmus test: **"How many zeroes got added to the enumerated state space because of this choice?"** If the answer isn't zero, the change requires explicit justification.
1268	
1269	### Rules
1270	
1271	1. **One canonical form**: Every command, argument, and parameter has exactly one accepted form. Do not accept alternatives.
1272	2. **No tolerances**: Do not add case folding, prefix stripping, alias acceptance, or alternative parse paths. If the canonical form is `--racing`, do not also accept `-r`, `racing`, or `RACING`.
1273	3. **No undocumented defaults**: Do not add `${1:-default}` patterns that silently fill missing required arguments. Required means required.
1274	4. **No silent normalization**: Do not silently transform input. If input doesn't match the canonical form, `buc_die` with a clear message stating the expected form.
1275	5. **Documentation uses canonical form**: All documentation, examples, and `buc_doc_*` output must show the single canonical invocation — never a tolerated alternative.
1276	
1277	### Smell Test
1278	
1279	If a command can be invoked two different ways for the same result, the interface is contaminated. If `"${1:-}"` silently provides a default for what should be a mandatory argument, the interface is contaminated.
1280	
1281	---
1282	
1283	## `set -e` is Not Sufficient
1284	
1285	**The POSIX suppression rule**: `set -e` is suppressed inside `if`, `while`, `||`, `&&` test expressions, and this propagates through the **entire call tree** of the tested command.
1286	
1287	```bash
1288	# ❌ set -e suppressed for entire call tree of some_function
1289	if some_function; then ...
1290	
1291	# Even if some_function calls other_function that fails,
1292	# set -e will not terminate — suppression propagates
1293	```
1294	
1295	**BCG rule: Only `_predicate` functions may appear in `if`/`while` conditions.** All other functions must be invoked as simple commands with explicit `|| buc_die` / `|| return`. This completely prevents the suppression hazard.
1296	
1297	```bash
1298	# ✅ Predicate in conditional — designed for this, never dies, status only
1299	if z«prefix»_ready_predicate; then ...
1300	
1301	# ✅ Regular function — explicit error handling, not inside conditional
1302	some_function || buc_die "..."
1303	
1304	# ❌ Regular function in conditional — set -e suppressed for entire call tree
1305	if some_function; then ...
1306	```
1307	
1308	**Why BCG's `|| buc_die` discipline works**: It doesn't rely on `set -e` — explicit error handling after every command means suppression doesn't matter. The `|| z_status=$?` capture pattern intentionally relies on this suppression behavior.
1309	
1310	**Loops**: `set -e` does not reliably catch failures inside `for`/`while` loop bodies in bash 3.2. Every command in a loop body must have explicit error handling.
1311	
1312	The error handling suffix depends on the function type:
1313	
1314	| Function type    | On failure use       | Why                                             |
1315	|------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
1316	| Regular/enroll   | `\|\| buc_die`       | Enroll validates invariants; violations are fatal |
1317	| Predicate        | `\|\| return 1`      | Never dies, status only                          |
1318	| Capture/recite   | `\|\| return 1`      | Never dies, caller decides                       |
1319	| Yawp             | N/A                  | Cannot fail — pure builtins only                 |
1320	| Flow control     | `\|\| continue`      | Intentional skip to next iteration               |
1321	
1322	### ❌ Test-and-control-flow with `&&`
1323	
1324	```bash
1325	# ❌ Relies on set -e exemption for &&/|| lists — fragile
1326	test "${z_val}" = "done" && break
1327	test -z "${z_val}" && return 0
1328	[[ "${z_line}" == *"pattern"* ]] && continue
1329	
1330	# ✅ Invert the test, use || — same behavior, no exemption reliance
1331	test "${z_val}" != "done" || break
1332	test -n "${z_val}" || return 0
1333	[[ "${z_line}" != *"pattern"* ]] || continue
1334	```
1335	
1336	**BCG rule: Never `test ... && break/continue/return`.** Always invert the test and use `||`. The `||` form is already the BCG standard for control flow after a test; the `&&` form relies on humans remembering that bash exempts `&&`/`||` lists from `set -e`, which is a language-lawyer trap.
1337	
1338	### ❌ `A && B || C` as Pseudo-Ternary
1339	
1340	```bash
1341	# ❌ C runs both when A fails AND when B fails — not equivalent to if/then/else
1342	curl "${z_url}" && jq '.field' "${z_file}" || buc_die "Failed"
1343	
1344	# ✅ Explicit if/then/else — C runs only when A fails
1345	if curl "${z_url}"; then
1346	  jq '.field' "${z_file}" || buc_die "jq failed"
1347	else
1348	  buc_die "curl failed"
1349	fi
1350	```
1351	
1352	**BCG rule: Never `A && B || C`.** This is not if-then-else. When `A` succeeds but `B` fails, `C` runs — which is almost never the intended behavior. Use explicit `if`/`then`/`else` for conditional execution with a fallback.
1353	
1354	---
1355	
1356	## Array Safety Under `set -u`
1357	
1358	Under `set -u`, `"${array[@]}"` on an empty array triggers "unbound variable" in bash 3.2. Two patterns are safe; choose by whether you need the index.
1359	
1360	**Index iteration** — use when you need the index (parallel arrays, kindle rolls). Inherently safe on empty arrays, no guard needed:
1361	
1362	```bash
1363	for z_i in "${!z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
1364	  echo "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[$z_i]}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
1365	done
1366	```
1367	
1368	**Guarded value iteration** — use when you only need values. The `(( ))` guard prevents expansion of the empty array:
1369	
1370	```bash
1371	if (( ${#z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]} )); then
1372	  for z_val in "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
1373	    echo "${z_val}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
1374	  done
1375	fi
1376	```
1377	
1378	**Anti-pattern** — unguarded value iteration on a possibly-empty array:
1379	
1380	```bash
1381	# ❌ Crashes under set -u in bash 3.2 when array is empty
1382	for z_val in "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
1383	  echo "${z_val}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
1384	done
1385	```
1386	
1387	---
1388	
1389	## Stdin Consumption in While-Read Loops
1390	
1391	### The Problem
1392	
1393	When `while read ... done < file` feeds a file to stdin (FD 0), **any child process that reads stdin silently consumes the loop's remaining input**. This includes `docker exec -i`, `ssh`, `read` without explicit FD, `cat` without arguments, and any command that inherits stdin.
1394	
1395	The failure is **silent and partial**: the loop processes some iterations correctly, then stops early with no error. This is one of the hardest bash bugs to diagnose because the loop appears to work — it just processes fewer items than expected.
1396	
1397	### ❌ Anti-Pattern: Open-FD Loop Body
1398	
1399	```bash
1400	# ❌ File held open on stdin for entire loop — child processes can consume it
1401	while IFS= read -r z_item || test -n "${z_item}"; do
1402	    complex_function "${z_item}"    # If this touches stdin, remaining items vanish
1403	done < "${z_temp_file}"
1404	```
1405	
1406	### ✅ Pattern: Load-Then-Iterate
1407	
1408	**Always** read file contents into an array first, then iterate. The file is fully consumed and closed before any loop body code runs.
1409	
1410	```bash
1411	# ✅ Load phase — file consumed and closed
1412	local z_items=()
1413	while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
1414	    z_items+=("${z_line}")
1415	done < "${z_temp_file}"
1416	rm -f "${z_temp_file}"
1417	
1418	# ✅ Iterate phase — stdin is free, no FD held open
1419	local z_i
1420	for z_i in "${!z_items[@]}"; do
1421	    test -n "${z_items[$z_i]}" || continue
1422	    complex_function "${z_items[$z_i]}"
1423	done
1424	```
1425	
1426	---
1427	
1428	## Sourcing Rules
1429	
1430	Sourcing is restricted because it breaks error handling. Only three locations may source files:
1431	
1432	| Location             | Can Source                 | Purpose                                              |
1433	|----------------------|----------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
1434	| CLI file header      | `buc_command.sh` only      | Bootstrap command infrastructure                     |
1435	| CLI Furnish Function | All deps + config files    | Module loading, environment setup                    |
1436	| Internal functions   | Credentials when necessary | Context-specific secrets (with documented rationale) |
1437	
1438	The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh` (via `BURD_BUK_DIR`). All other module and config sourcing happens inside the furnish function, after the `buc_doc_env` / `buc_doc_env_done` gate. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime dependencies.
1439	
1440	## Eval Policy
1441	
1442	`eval` is **forbidden** except for validated variable-name dereference. Require `^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$` regex validation before any eval.
1443	
1444	**Prefer indirect expansion for reading** — `${!name}` works in bash 3.2:
1445	
1446	```bash
1447	# ✅ Indirect expansion for reading
1448	local z_val="${!z_varname}"
1449	```
1450	
1451	**Use `printf -v` for assignment to indirect variable** — after name validation:
1452	
1453	```bash
1454	# ✅ printf -v for assignment — no quoting hazards from values
1455	echo "${z_varname}" | grep -qE '^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$' \
1456	  || buc_die "Invalid variable name: ${z_varname}"
1457	printf -v "${z_varname}" '%s' "${z_new_value}" || buc_die "printf -v failed"
1458	```
1459	
1460	**Anti-patterns:**
1461	
1462	```bash
1463	# ❌ eval for assignment — quoting hazards from values with quotes/newlines/backslashes
1464	eval "${z_varname}=\${z_new_value}"
1465	
1466	# ❌ Unvalidated eval — injection risk
1467	eval "local z_val=\${${z_varname}:-}"
1468	```
1469	
1470	## Command Dependency Discipline
1471	
1472	External commands are potential failure points, portability hazards, and supply-chain surface area. Every command invocation outside of bash builtins must earn its place.
1473	
1474	### POSIX Utility Allowlist
1475	
1476	These external commands are accepted in any BUK-based project. Each has no bash 3.2 builtin replacement and is mandated by POSIX on any system that runs bash:
1477	
1478	`chmod`, `cp`, `date`, `find`, `mkdir`, `mktemp`, `mv`, `rm`, `sed`, `sleep`, `sort`, `stty`
1479	
1480	Commands on this list need no justification — they are the irreducible dependency floor.
1481	
1482	### Evicted Utilities
1483	
1484	These commands have bash builtin or declared-dependency replacements. Do not reintroduce them.
1485	
1486	| Evicted | Replacement | Notes |
1487	|---------|-------------|-------|
1488	| `awk` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Field splitting is a builtin |
1489	| `base64` | `openssl enc -base64` | Platform flags differ (GNU `-d` vs BSD `-D`) |
1490	| `cut` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Same as awk — field extraction is a builtin |
1491	| `grep` | `case`, `test`, `[[ =~ ]]` | BCG-blessed pattern matching; see Test vs Bracket Expressions |
1492	| `head` | `read -r` | First-line extraction is a single builtin call |
1493	| `ls` | Glob expansion | `for f in dir/*` iterates without spawning a process |
1494	| `sha256sum`/`shasum` | `openssl dgst -sha256 -r` | Platform names differ; openssl is universal |
1495	| `tr` | `${var//old/new}` parameter expansion | Character replacement is a builtin |
1496	| `wc` | `${#var}`, `${#arr[@]}` | Length measurement is a builtin |
1497	
1498	### Declared Dependency Principle
1499

file.content:
### ✅ Correct Patterns

| Element                      | Pattern                      | Example                      | Location | Name/ Case Constraints        |
|------------------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|----------|-------------------------------|
| Module prefix                | `[a-z]{2,4}_`                | `rbv_`, `buc_`, `auth_`      | Both     | single char group             |
| Implementation file          | `«prefix»_«name».sh`         | `rbv_podman.sh`              | N/A      | lowercase (prefer 1 word, allow snake_case) |
| CLI file                     | `«prefix»_cli.sh`            | `rbv_cli.sh`                 | N/A      | snake_case (fixed)            |
| Public functions             | `«prefix»_«command»`         | `rbv_init`, `rbv_start`      | Impl     | snake_case (usually one word) |
| Internal functions           | `z«prefix»_«name»`           | `zrbv_validate_pat`          | Impl     | snake_case (often multi word) |
| Furnish function             | `z«prefix»_furnish`          | `zrbv_furnish`               | CLI      | fixed name                    |
| Kindle function              | `z«prefix»_kindle`           | `zrbv_kindle`                | Impl     | fixed name                    |
| Sentinel function            | `z«prefix»_sentinel`         | `zrbv_sentinel`              | Impl     | fixed name                    |
| Predicate Internal functions | `z«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `zrbv_file_exists_predicate` | Impl     | snake_case                    |
| Capture Internal functions   | `z«prefix»_«name»_capture`   | `zrbv_get_token_capture`     | Impl     | snake_case                    |
| Capture Public functions     | `«prefix»_«name»_capture`    | `rbv_get_token_capture`      | Impl     | snake_case                    |
| Kindle constant (internal)   | `Z«PREFIX»_«NAME»`           | `ZRBV_TEMP_FILE`             | Impl     | SCREAMING_SNAKE (multi-word)  |
| Kindle constant (public)     | `«PREFIX»_«NAME»`            | `RBV_REGIME_FILE`            | Both     | SCREAMING_SNAKE (multi-word)  |
| Tinder constant (public)     | `«PREFIX»_«name»`            | `RBBC_rbrr_file`             | Impl     | lower_snake (multi-word)      |
| Local parameters             | `z_«name»`                   | `z_vm_name`, `z_force_flag`  | Both     | snake_case (multi-word)       |
| Enroll functions             | `[z]«prefix»_[«scope»_]enroll` | `«prefix»_enroll`          | Impl     | kindle-only                   |
| Recite functions             | `[z]«prefix»_«what»_recite`  | `«prefix»_target_recite`     | Impl     | read-only, never mutates      |
| Roll arrays                  | `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll`     | `z_rbv_target_roll`          | Impl     | snake_case, kindle-only       |
| Enroll return vars           | `z_«funcname»_«retval»`      | `z_«prefix»_enroll_name`     | Impl     | snake_case (func name verbatim)|
| Testbench file               | `«prefix»tb_testbench.sh`    | `rbtb_testbench.sh`          | N/A      | fixed name                    |
| Test case file               | `«prefix»tc«xx»_«Name».sh`  | `rbtckk_KickTires.sh`        | N/A      | PascalCase name               |
| Fixture baste function       | `z«tb»_«name»_baste`        | `zrbtb_ark_baste`            | Testbench| snake_case, fixture layer     |
| Case function                | `«tc»_«name»_tcase`         | `rbtckk_false_tcase`         | Test case| snake_case, case layer        |
| Yawp function (public)       | `«prefix»_«name»_yawp`      | `buyy_cmd_yawp`              | Impl     | snake_case                    |
| Yawp function (internal)     | `z«prefix»_«name»_yawp`     | `zbuyf_resolve_yawp`         | Impl     | snake_case                    |
| Yawp return variable         | `z_«module»_«group»`        | `z_buym_yelp`                | Impl     | snake_case, mutable kindle state |
