#!/bin/bash
# shellcheck disable=SC2153  # kindle chain - per BCG
#
# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Bash Utility Regime Dispatch - Direct bash dispatch

set -euo pipefail

BURE_VERBOSE=${BURE_VERBOSE:-0}

BURD_REGIME_FILE=${BURD_REGIME_FILE:-"__MISSING_BURD_REGIME_FILE__"}

# Utility function for verbose output
zbud_show() { test "$BURE_VERBOSE" != "1" || echo "BURDSHOW: $*"; }

# Enable trace mode if verbose level is 2
if test "${BURE_VERBOSE}" = "2"; then
  set -x
fi

zbud_die() { echo "FATAL: $*" >&2; exit 1; }

zburd_sentinel() {
  test "${ZBURD_INITIALIZED:-}" = "1" || zbud_die "Dispatch not initialized - zbud_main not complete"
}

# String validator with optional length constraints
zbud_check_string() {
  local -r z_context="${1}"
  local -r z_varname="${2}"
  local z_val="${!z_varname:-}"
  local -r z_min="${3}"
  local -r z_max="${4}"

  test "${z_min}" != "0" -o -n "${z_val}" || return 0
  test -n "${z_val}" || zbud_die "[${z_context}] ${z_varname} must not be empty"

  if test -n "${z_max}"; then
    test ${#z_val} -ge "${z_min}" || zbud_die "[${z_context}] ${z_varname} must be at least ${z_min} chars, got '${z_val}' (${#z_val})"
    test ${#z_val} -le "${z_max}" || zbud_die "[${z_context}] ${z_varname} must be no more than ${z_max} chars, got '${z_val}' (${#z_val})"
  fi
}

# Source configuration and setup environment
zbud_setup() {
  zbud_show "Starting BDU setup"

  source            "${BURD_REGIME_FILE}"

  # Apply BURV (Bash Utility Regime Verification) overrides if set
  BURC_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR="${BURV_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR:-${BURC_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR}}"
  BURC_TEMP_ROOT_DIR="${BURV_TEMP_ROOT_DIR:-${BURC_TEMP_ROOT_DIR}}"

  zbud_check_string "${BURD_REGIME_FILE}" BURC_STATION_FILE        1 256
  zbud_check_string "${BURD_REGIME_FILE}" BURC_LOG_LAST            1 256
  zbud_check_string "${BURD_REGIME_FILE}" BURC_LOG_EXT             1 32
  zbud_check_string "${BURD_REGIME_FILE}" BURC_TABTARGET_DIR       1 256
  zbud_check_string "${BURD_REGIME_FILE}" BURC_TABTARGET_DELIMITER 1 8
  zbud_check_string "${BURD_REGIME_FILE}" BURC_TEMP_ROOT_DIR       1 256
  zbud_check_string "${BURD_REGIME_FILE}" BURC_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR     1 256
  zbud_check_string "${BURD_REGIME_FILE}" BURC_TOOLS_DIR           1 256

  # Dispatch-provided directory variables (survive exec boundary for CLIs)
  BURD_TOOLS_DIR="${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}"
  BURD_BUK_DIR="${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}/buk"
  BURD_TABTARGET_DIR="${BURC_TABTARGET_DIR}"
  export BURD_TOOLS_DIR BURD_BUK_DIR BURD_TABTARGET_DIR

  # Source station file (skip for no-log handbook tabtargets)
  if test -z "${BURD_NO_LOG:-}"; then
    zbud_show "Sourcing station file: ${BURC_STATION_FILE}"
    source                           "${BURC_STATION_FILE}"

    # Validate station variables
    zbud_check_string "${BURC_STATION_FILE}" BURS_LOG_DIR 1 256
  fi

  mkdir -p "${BURC_TEMP_ROOT_DIR}" || zbud_die "Failed to create temp root: ${BURC_TEMP_ROOT_DIR}"
  local -r z_date_file="${BURC_TEMP_ROOT_DIR}/bud_bootstrap_date.txt"
  date +'%Y%m%d-%H%M%S %s' > "${z_date_file}" || zbud_die "Failed to get datetime"
  local z_datetime
  z_datetime=$(<"${z_date_file}")
  test -n "${z_datetime}" || zbud_die "Empty datetime from ${z_date_file}"
  BURD_NOW_STAMP="${z_datetime% *}-$$-$((RANDOM % 1000))"
  BURD_NOW_EPOCH="${z_datetime#* }"
  zbud_show "Generated timestamp: ${BURD_NOW_STAMP} epoch: ${BURD_NOW_EPOCH}"

  BURD_TEMP_DIR="${BURC_TEMP_ROOT_DIR}/temp-${BURD_NOW_STAMP}"
  case "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}" in
    /*) ;;
    *)  BURD_TEMP_DIR="${PWD}/${BURD_TEMP_DIR}" ;;
  esac
  mkdir -p                           "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}" || zbud_die "Failed to create temp directory: ${BURD_TEMP_DIR}"
  zbud_show "Generated temporary dir: ${BURD_TEMP_DIR}"

  # Setup transcript file path
  BURD_TRANSCRIPT="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/transcript.txt"

  # Setup output directory (fixed location, cleared on each run)
  BURD_OUTPUT_DIR="${BURC_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR}/current"
  case "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}" in
    /*) ;;
    *)  BURD_OUTPUT_DIR="${PWD}/${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}" ;;
  esac

  # Clear if exists, then create fresh
  if test -d "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"; then
    zbud_show "Clearing existing output directory: ${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"
    rm -rf "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}" || zbud_die "Failed to remove output directory: ${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"
  fi
  mkdir -p "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}" || zbud_die "Failed to create output directory: ${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"

  zbud_show "Output directory ready: ${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"

  # Get Git context
  local -r z_git_context_file="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bud_git_context.txt"
  local -r z_git_context_stderr="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bud_git_context_stderr.txt"
  if git describe --always --dirty --tags --long > "${z_git_context_file}" 2>"${z_git_context_stderr}"; then
    BURD_GIT_CONTEXT=$(<"${z_git_context_file}")
  else
    BURD_GIT_CONTEXT="git-unavailable"
  fi
  zbud_show "Git context: ${BURD_GIT_CONTEXT}"

  # Export for child processes
  export BURD_TEMP_DIR
  export BURD_OUTPUT_DIR
  export BURD_NOW_STAMP
  export BURD_NOW_EPOCH
  export BURD_TRANSCRIPT
  export BURD_GIT_CONTEXT

  return 0
}

# Process command-line arguments
zbud_process_args() {
  local -r z_target="${1}"
  shift

  zbud_show "Processing target: ${z_target}"

  # Extract tokens from tabtarget
  local -a z_tokens
  IFS="${BURC_TABTARGET_DELIMITER}" read -ra z_tokens <<< "${z_target}"
  zbud_show "Split tokens: ${z_tokens[*]}"

  # Store primary command token (legacy, equivalent to BURD_TOKEN_1)
  BURD_COMMAND="${z_tokens[0]}"

  # Explode tokens into numbered variables for workbench access
  # Pattern matches MBC_TTPARAM__FIRST through MBC_TTPARAM__FIFTH
  BURD_TOKEN_1="${z_tokens[0]:-}"
  BURD_TOKEN_2="${z_tokens[1]:-}"
  BURD_TOKEN_3="${z_tokens[2]:-}"
  BURD_TOKEN_4="${z_tokens[3]:-}"
  BURD_TOKEN_5="${z_tokens[4]:-}"

  export BURD_TOKEN_1 BURD_TOKEN_2 BURD_TOKEN_3 BURD_TOKEN_4 BURD_TOKEN_5

  # Create tag for log files
  local -r z_tag="${z_tokens[0]}-${z_tokens[2]:-unknown}"

  # Setup log paths and files (skip entirely for no-log tabtargets)
  if test -z "${BURD_NO_LOG:-}"; then
    BURD_LOG_LAST="${BURS_LOG_DIR}/${BURC_LOG_LAST}.${BURC_LOG_EXT}"
    BURD_LOG_SAME="${BURS_LOG_DIR}/same-${z_tag}.${BURC_LOG_EXT}"
    BURD_LOG_HIST="${BURS_LOG_DIR}/hist-${z_tag}-${BURD_NOW_STAMP}.${BURC_LOG_EXT}"
    mkdir -p "${BURS_LOG_DIR}"
    : > "${BURD_LOG_LAST}"
    : > "${BURD_LOG_SAME}"
    : > "${BURD_LOG_HIST}"
  fi

  # Store target and extra arguments
  BURD_TARGET="${z_target}"
  BURD_CLI_ARGS=("$@")

  # Export command context for workbench access
  export BURD_COMMAND
  export BURD_TARGET
  export BURD_CLI_ARGS

  return 0
}

# Function to curate logs for the 'same' log file (normalized output)
zbud_curate_same() {
  # Convert to unix line endings, strip colors, normalize temp dir, remove VOLATILE lines
  sed -e 's/\r/\n/g'                             \
      -e '/^$/d'                                 \
      -e 's/\x1b[\[][0-9;]*[a-zA-Z]//g'          \
      -e 's/\x1b[(][A-Z]//g'                     \
      -e "s|${BURD_TEMP_DIR}|BURD_EPHEMERAL_DIR|g" \
      -e '/VOLATILE/d'
}

# Function to curate logs for the historical log file (with timestamps)
zbud_curate_hist() {
  while read -r z_line; do
    printf "[%s] %s\n" "$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")" "${z_line}"
  done
}

# Generate and log checksum for a file
zbud_generate_checksum() {
  local -r z_file="${1}"
  local -r z_output_file="${2}"

  local z_checksum
  z_checksum=$(openssl dgst -sha256 -r "${z_file}" 2>/dev/null) || z_checksum="checksum-unavailable"
  read -r z_checksum _ <<< "${z_checksum}"

  echo "Same log checksum: ${z_checksum}" >> "${z_output_file}"
  return 0
}

# Resolve color policy once at dispatch time and export BURE_COLOR (0/1)
zbud_resolve_color() {
  if test -n "${NO_COLOR:-}"; then
    export BURE_COLOR=0
    return 0
  fi
  case "${BURE_COLOR:-auto}" in
    0|1)
      export BURE_COLOR
      ;;
    auto|*)
      if test -t 1 && test "${TERM:-}" != "dumb"; then
          export BURE_COLOR=1
      else
          export BURE_COLOR=0
      fi
      ;;
  esac
}

# Write nanosecond-precision timestamp to the file specified by $1
zbud_nanosecond_timestamp() {
  local -r z_output_file="${1}"
  if command -v gdate >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    gdate +'%Y%m%d-%H%M%S.%N' > "${z_output_file}" || zbud_die "gdate failed"
  else
    date +'%Y%m%d-%H%M%S.000000000' > "${z_output_file}" || zbud_die "date failed"
  fi
}

zbud_write_burx_initial() {
  local -r z_ts_file="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/zbud_began_at.txt"
  zbud_nanosecond_timestamp "${z_ts_file}"
  local -r z_began_at=$(<"${z_ts_file}")
  test -n "${z_began_at}" || zbud_die "Empty began_at timestamp"

  local -r z_label="${BURE_LABEL:-}"
  local -r z_content="BURX_PID=$$
BURX_BEGAN_AT=${z_began_at}
BURX_TABTARGET=${BURD_TARGET}
BURX_TEMP_DIR=${BURD_TEMP_DIR}
BURX_TRANSCRIPT=${BURD_TRANSCRIPT}
BURX_LOG_HIST=${BURD_LOG_HIST:-}
BURX_LABEL=${z_label}"
  buf_write_fact_single "${BUF_burx_env}" "${z_content}"
}

zbud_write_burx_completion() {
  local -r z_exit_status="${1}"
  local -r z_ts_file="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/zbud_ended_at.txt"
  zbud_nanosecond_timestamp "${z_ts_file}"
  local -r z_ended_at=$(<"${z_ts_file}")
  test -n "${z_ended_at}" || zbud_die "Empty ended_at timestamp"

  printf 'BURX_EXIT_STATUS=%s\nBURX_ENDED_AT=%s\n' "${z_exit_status}" "${z_ended_at}" >> "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${BUF_burx_env}"
  printf 'BURX_EXIT_STATUS=%s\nBURX_ENDED_AT=%s\n' "${z_exit_status}" "${z_ended_at}" >> "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/${BUF_burx_env}"
}

zbud_main() {
  zbud_show "Starting BDU dispatch"

  # Decide color policy before stdout is piped
  zbud_resolve_color

  # Setup environment
  zbud_setup || { echo "ERROR: Environment setup failed" >&2; exit 1; }
  zbud_show "Environment setup complete"

  # Source fact-file module for BURX writes
  source "${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}/buk/buf_fact.sh"

  # Process arguments
  zbud_process_args "$@" || { echo "ERROR: Argument processing failed" >&2; exit 1; }
  zbud_show "Arguments processed"

  # Write initial BURX exchange state
  zbud_write_burx_initial

  # Detect unexpected BURD_ variables
  local -r z_known="BURD_CONFIG_DIR BURD_REGIME_FILE BURD_NO_LOG BURD_INTERACTIVE BURD_COORDINATOR_SCRIPT BURD_LAUNCHER BURD_STATION_FILE BURD_TERM_COLS BURD_NOW_STAMP BURD_NOW_EPOCH BURD_TEMP_DIR BURD_OUTPUT_DIR BURD_TRANSCRIPT BURD_GIT_CONTEXT BURD_LOG_LAST BURD_LOG_SAME BURD_LOG_HIST BURD_COMMAND BURD_TARGET BURD_CLI_ARGS BURD_TOKEN_1 BURD_TOKEN_2 BURD_TOKEN_3 BURD_TOKEN_4 BURD_TOKEN_5 BURD_TOOLS_DIR BURD_BUK_DIR BURD_TABTARGET_DIR"
  ZBURD_UNEXPECTED=()
  local z_var
  for z_var in $(compgen -v BURD_); do
    case " ${z_known} " in
      *" ${z_var} "*) : ;;
      *) ZBURD_UNEXPECTED+=("${z_var}") ;;
    esac
  done

  # Die on unexpected variables
  if test ${#ZBURD_UNEXPECTED[@]} -gt 0; then
    zbud_die "Unexpected BURD_ variables: ${ZBURD_UNEXPECTED[*]}"
  fi

  ZBURD_INITIALIZED=1

  # Build complete invocation array (always has ≥2 elements, so always safe under set -u)
  local -r z_coordinator_cmd="${BURD_COORDINATOR_SCRIPT}"
  local -a z_invocation=("${z_coordinator_cmd}" "${BURD_COMMAND}")
  if test ${#BURD_CLI_ARGS[@]} -gt 0; then
    z_invocation+=("${BURD_CLI_ARGS[@]}")
  fi
  zbud_show "Coordinator command: ${z_invocation[*]}"

  # Log command to all log files (or suppress all output if BURD_NO_LOG)
  if test -z "${BURD_NO_LOG:-}"; then
    if test -n "${BURD_INTERACTIVE:-}"; then
      echo "log (interactive): ${BURD_LOG_HIST}"
      echo "command: ${z_invocation[*]}" >> "${BURD_LOG_HIST}"
      echo "Git context: ${BURD_GIT_CONTEXT}"  >> "${BURD_LOG_HIST}"
    else
      echo "log files:   ${BURD_LOG_LAST} ${BURD_LOG_SAME} ${BURD_LOG_HIST}"
      echo "command: ${z_invocation[*]}" >> "${BURD_LOG_LAST}"
      echo "command: ${z_invocation[*]}" >> "${BURD_LOG_SAME}"
      echo "command: ${z_invocation[*]}" >> "${BURD_LOG_HIST}"
      echo "Git context: ${BURD_GIT_CONTEXT}"  >> "${BURD_LOG_HIST}"
    fi
    echo "transcript:  ${BURD_TRANSCRIPT}"
    echo "output dir:  ${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"
  fi

  zbud_show "Executing coordinator"

  # Execute coordinator with logging
  set +e
  zBURD_STATUS_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/status-$$"
  if test -n "${BURD_INTERACTIVE:-}" && test -z "${BURD_NO_LOG:-}"; then
    # Interactive mode with logging: uncurated tee to historical log, preserves line buffering
    "${z_invocation[@]}" 2>&1 | tee -a "${BURD_LOG_HIST}"
    zBURD_EXIT_STATUS=${PIPESTATUS[0]}
    echo "${zBURD_EXIT_STATUS}" > "${zBURD_STATUS_FILE}"
    zbud_show "Coordinator status (interactive): ${zBURD_EXIT_STATUS}"
  elif test -n "${BURD_NO_LOG:-}"; then
    {
      "${z_invocation[@]}"
      echo $? > "${zBURD_STATUS_FILE}"
      zbud_show "Coordinator status: $(cat "${zBURD_STATUS_FILE}")"
    }
  else
    {
      "${z_invocation[@]}" 2>&1
      echo $? > "${zBURD_STATUS_FILE}"
      zbud_show "Coordinator status: $(cat "${zBURD_STATUS_FILE}")"
    } | while IFS= read -r z_line; do
        printf '%s\n' "${z_line}" >> "${BURD_LOG_LAST}"
        printf '%s\n' "${z_line}" | zbud_curate_same >> "${BURD_LOG_SAME}"
        printf '%s\n' "${z_line}" | zbud_curate_hist >> "${BURD_LOG_HIST}"
        printf '%s\n' "${z_line}"  # to stdout
      done
  fi

  zBURD_EXIT_STATUS=$(cat "${zBURD_STATUS_FILE}")
  rm                     "${zBURD_STATUS_FILE}"

  # Write BURX completion state
  zbud_write_burx_completion "${zBURD_EXIT_STATUS}"

  set -e

  # Generate checksum for the log files (only when enabled)
  if test -z "${BURD_NO_LOG:-}"; then
    zbud_generate_checksum "${BURD_LOG_SAME}" "${BURD_LOG_HIST}"
    zbud_show "Checksum generated"
  fi

  zbud_show "BDU completed with status: ${zBURD_EXIT_STATUS}"

  exit "${zBURD_EXIT_STATUS}"
}

zbud_main "$@"

# eof

lastPrompt: Read BCG to learn the bash coding requirements I have.  Read BUK dispatch code to see how _TEMP_DIR works- its where you will place temp files.  Ask any questions we should answer before slating given…

content.text:
Read both. The BCG and dispatch reveal real surface area I want to nail down. Five questions before slating:

**Q1 — Kindle-time tripwire scope: which CLIs actually call the check, when?**

The BCG regime archetype puts validation in `enforce`, then locks variables readonly. If the tripwire fires inside `zrbrd_enforce`, *every* operation that loads RBRD pays a `docker pull + create + cp + rm + diff` round-trip — including `rbrd_render`/`rbrd_validate` which are pure-config commands and should stay light per the BCG **differential furnish** pattern.

Options:
- **(a)** Tripwire inside `zrbrd_enforce` — universal protection, but render/validate become heavyweight
- **(b)** Tripwire as a separate public function `rbrd_check` that consuming CLI furnishes call after kindling RBRD. Differential-furnish style: `rbrd_validate`/`rbrd_render` skip the check; `rbf_*`, `rbg*_*`, anything doing GCP work runs it
- **(c)** Something else

I'd lean (b) for BCG fit. Your call.

**Q2 — Who writes the RBRD image at levy, and how?**

Levy is a Payor operation. At that point Director/Retriever SAs don't exist yet. Three real paths:
- **(a)** Payor host-side: `docker login -u oauth2accesstoken --password-stdin` using Payor's OAuth access token, then host `docker build` + `docker push`. Mirrors the Pouch pattern but at levy-time.
- **(b)** Cloud Build step inside the existing levy flow, mirroring `zrbgp_pool_probe_submit` at `rbgp_Payor.sh:527`. Probe pattern is established; one more cloud build step adds the RBRD push. No host docker for the write side; heredocs inside the build script remain BCG-legal (cloud-side bash exception noted in `rbgp_Payor.sh:543-544`).
- **(c)** Deferred: levy doesn't push; first Director command after levy pushes idempotently if absent. Risky — kindle-time tripwire fires before the image exists on first use.

I'd lean (b) — consistent with the probes, no new host-side push mechanism needed at levy time. But (a) is also clean.

**Q3 — Whose credentials does the tripwire pull use?**

The kindle-time pull needs GAR read. Every role (Director, Retriever, Governor) already has GAR read for their normal work. The natural pattern is "whichever role is operating now uses its own token." That fits the existing `rbfr_FoundryRetriever.sh:168` and `rbob_bottle.sh:263` pattern — same `docker login -u oauth2accesstoken --password-stdin` form, the consuming CLI passes the token it already has.

Confirm this is the model? (Alternative: always pull as Retriever — adds a credential dependency on commands that don't otherwise need Retriever creds.)

**Q4 — What lives in `rbgp_Payor.sh` vs new modules?**

Two new pieces of code: the RBRD image write (levy-time) and the RBRD image read+diff (kindle-time). Natural homes:
- **Write** in `rbgp_Payor.sh` near the existing depot-fact refresh hook (`zrbgp_depot_list_update` at line 301) — feels right since levy already lives there.
- **Read+diff** in `rbrd_regime.sh` as `rbrd_check` public function (per Q1 option b). Takes a token, does docker pull/create/cp/rm/diff with full BCG hygiene (kindle constants for temp paths, `2>"${ZRBRD_*_STDERR}"` capture, `|| buc_die "... — see <stderr>"`).

Does this division feel right, or should the write live somewhere else (e.g., a new tiny module or alongside the read code)?

**Q5 — One mechanical confirm: heredocs.**

The cloud-build script generation in `rbgp_Payor.sh:546` uses `printf '%s\n' ...` to assemble the cloud-side bash, with cloud-side heredocs allowed inside the assembled script per the existing exception. The host-side Dockerfile (if Q2 = (a), host-side push) would also need `printf '%s\n' 'FROM scratch' 'COPY rbrd.env /' > "${ZRBGP_RBRD_DOCKERFILE}"` rather than a heredoc. Confirm this is understood for the plan — no host-side heredocs in any new code.

---

**Things the BCG reading confirmed without ambiguity (no question needed, just listing so you can flag if I've misread):**
- Temp paths get kindle constants like `ZRBGP_RBRD_DOCKERFILE`, `ZRBGP_RBRD_PULL_STDERR`, etc. All under `BURD_TEMP_DIR`. Forensic-preserved, never deleted in module code.
- Every external command (docker, openssl) gets stderr captured to a temp file path that appears in the `buc_die` message.
- New regime module `rbrd_regime.sh` follows the regime archetype: `buv_*_enroll` per field in kindle, `buv_scope_sentinel` last, `zrbrd_enforce` calls `buv_vet RBRD` plus custom format checks, then `readonly RBRD_*` lock.
- `rbrd_cli.sh` provides `rbrd_validate` (uses `buv_report`) and `rbrd_render` (uses `buv_render`).
- Tabtargets `rbw-rdr`/`rbw-rdv` enrolled in `rbw_workbench.sh` parallel to `rbw-rrr`/`rbw-rrv`.
- The image-extract pattern is exactly what's at `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:680` — docker create + docker cp + docker rm — adapted to host-side BCG discipline (kindle constants, stderr capture, two-line capture for `docker create` output).
- Diff at kindle uses the rbrd.env file path verbatim (from `RBRD_REGIME_FILE` per the template pattern) — operator's exact bytes in, exact bytes out.

content: Goal: shape pace ₢BOAAB to pull depot-regime work (RBRD rename + GAR-image tripwire) forward into heat ₣BO. Current task: answering five clarifying questions before slating. Next action: you decide on Q1–Q5, then I draft the pace dockets. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content:
Q1) I'm drawn to a rbrd_check function defined in rbrd_cli.sh to implement the check with buc_die on fail, and we should call it before every cloud submission.  Help me decide whether we should re-pull from GAR each time- I suspect the typical image management will keep this lightweight if nothing has changed.  Right?

Q2) I'm without conviction how to keep this simple and robust: is it simpler to form the image and  push from host during the levy operation?  That file is small but I know passing info to the cloud build can be tricky.  I don't want this to be hard.  Any attempt to overwrite the cached RBRD should be a fatal.

Q3) Isn't it simplest if the only SA that pulls this is Retriever?  All our tabtargets are exclusive to a single SA type

Q4) rbrd_regime.sh needs to be very like other rbr*_regime.sh files and thus is a very bad place to put bespoke code; on the other hand, rbrd_cli.sh is appropriate for regime specific, application specific processing.  I'd prefer not to make payor more complex, but you'll have an opinion once you study the code and I want to hear it.

Q5) BCG is required for host side code, yes.  I mean it.

content.text:
**Q1 — `docker pull` cost when unchanged:** Yes, your intuition is correct. The OCI distribution flow is: `HEAD /v2/<repo>/manifests/<tag>` returns the digest; if local image already has that digest, docker says "Image is up to date" and pulls zero bytes. For a single-layer FROM-scratch image with the same content, that's one HEAD round-trip — typically 50-200ms to GAR. The `docker create + docker cp + docker rm` are local operations on a tiny image, also negligible. Worst case across the whole check, ~100-300ms cached / ~500ms-1s cold. Compared to a multi-minute cloud build that follows, the overhead is invisible.

There IS one operational note: if the user's `docker pull` fails for any reason (daemon down, network, GAR access), the check fails — which matches "fail loudly" exactly. That's a feature.

Decision locked: `rbrd_check` re-pulls before every cloud submission.

---

**Q2 — Levy-time write, simpler path:** Host-side from Payor is simpler. The cloud-build path requires baking `rbrd.env` content into a generated cloud-side script (heredoc allowed cloud-side, but the host code that *generates* the script has to walk the file via `printf '%s\n'` lines per BCG — fiddly for arbitrary file content). Host-side is three docker commands with auth we already do for Pouches.

Host-side flow at end of successful levy:
1. `docker login -u oauth2accesstoken --password-stdin` using Payor's OAuth access token (Payor has full project access at this point)
2. **Overwrite-guard:** `docker manifest inspect <ref>` — if exit 0 (image already present), `buc_die "RBRD already inscribed at <ref> — unmake first, then relevy"`. If exit non-zero (404 expected), proceed.
3. Build context dir in `BURD_TEMP_DIR`, copy `rbrd.env` in, generate Dockerfile via `printf '%s\n' 'FROM scratch' 'COPY rbrd.env /' > "${z_dockerfile}"`
4. `docker build -t <ref> <ctx>`
5. `docker push <ref>`

Each step has stderr captured to a temp file referenced in `buc_die` on failure. All under BCG.

About passing info to cloud builds being tricky — agreed, you've been bitten by it before, and the host-side path completely sidesteps that class of error.

---

**Q3 — Retriever-only vs each-role-pulls:** I want to push back gently here. You're right that tabtargets are single-SA-per-target, but I think that argues *for* each-role-pulls, not Retriever-only.

If only Retriever pulls, then Director tabtargets (which is where the cloud-submission check fires most — `rbw-fO`, `rbw-fA`, `rbw-fV`) face a dilemma:
- Either the Director tabtarget invokes Retriever credentials to do the check (now the tabtarget uses two SAs — breaks the single-SA rule)
- Or the check runs as a separate Retriever-context preflight before each Director tabtarget (operator burden + race conditions: Retriever-check could pass, RBRD edited between check and Director run, Director submits with drifted state)

Each-role-pulls is the natural fit for the single-SA discipline:
- Director tabtarget → uses Director creds for both check and cloud submission
- Retriever tabtarget → uses Retriever creds for both check and Retriever's own cloud work (Summon, Plumb)
- Each role already has GAR image-read for their normal pulls; no IAM additions

Concretely: each consuming CLI's furnish loads its role's RBRA credential as usual, gets the bearer token, calls `rbrd_check "${z_token}"`. The function is role-agnostic — it just needs *some* GAR-read token.

Want me to go with each-role-pulls, or are there reasons you've thought of for Retriever-only that I'm missing?

---

**Q4 — Where the bespoke code lives:** I agree with your framing of `rbrd_regime.sh` (uniform with siblings, no bespoke code) and `rbrd_cli.sh` (appropriate home for regime-specific application logic). I also agree with your preference not to grow `rbgp_Payor.sh`.

My recommendation: **both `rbrd_check` and `rbrd_inscribe` live as public functions in `rbrd_cli.sh`**, exposed as commands (`rbw-rdc.RbrdCheck.sh`, `rbw-rdi.RbrdInscribe.sh`). Differential furnish handles the dependency split cleanly:
- `rbrd_validate` / `rbrd_render` — light path, regime only
- `rbrd_check` — source RBRP (Payor creds for token? actually no — uses caller-supplied token, see below) + docker; loads enough to do pull+create+cp+rm+diff
- `rbrd_inscribe` — source RBRP (Payor auth) + docker; loads enough to do login+manifest-inspect+build+push

**How callers invoke `rbrd_check`:** the cleanest BCG-compliant pattern is the consuming CLI's furnish does `exec "${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/rbk/rbrd_cli.sh" rbrd_check` — but that's a fresh process per check. For inline use within an existing CLI's process, the BCG-licit options are:
- Tabtarget-level: `tt/rbw-fO.DirectorOrdainsHallmark.sh` calls `tt/rbw-rdc.RbrdCheck.sh` (or its workbench equivalent) as a preflight before the Director ordain
- CLI-level: A Director CLI sources `rbrd_cli.sh`'s `furnish` and calls `rbrd_check` directly (allowed per BCG: "Another CLI's furnish (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)")

Tabtarget-level is simpler and keeps each tabtarget single-SA per Q3. The Director tabtarget orchestrates: preflight `rbw-rdc`, then the Director cloud op. If the preflight dies, the cloud op never runs.

**Payor invocation of `rbrd_inscribe`:** at end of `rbw-dL` (PayorLeviesDepot), the levy tabtarget calls `rbw-rdi` (RbrdInscribe). One-line addition in the tabtarget, no growth in `rbgp_Payor.sh`.

This keeps `rbgp_Payor.sh` exactly where it is, makes `rbrd_cli.sh` the home for the bespoke RBRD ceremony, and respects the single-SA-per-tabtarget rule via the orchestrate-at-tabtarget pattern.

---

**Q5 — BCG required for host-side, I mean it:** Acknowledged unequivocally. No host-side heredocs anywhere. Dockerfile content via `printf '%s\n'`. Every external command (docker, openssl) has stderr to a kindle-constant temp path and that path appears in the `buc_die` message. All locals `local -r` except the four exceptions. Two-line capture pattern everywhere. No `local z_var=$(cmd)`. No `local -i`. No `[[ ]]` outside `=~`. No `&&`-then-control-flow. Temp file names use kindle constants like `ZRBRD_INSCRIBE_DOCKERFILE`, `ZRBRD_CHECK_PULL_STDERR`, `ZRBRD_CHECK_EXTRACT_STDERR`, `ZRBRD_INSCRIBE_MANIFEST_STDERR`, etc. All under `BURD_TEMP_DIR`. Forensic-preserved.

---

**Where this leaves us:** decomposition is now sharp enough to slate. Two open items from Q3 and Q4:

1. Confirm each-role-pulls (or push back if you want Retriever-only with a preflight-orchestration story)
2. Confirm the tabtarget-orchestration pattern for `rbrd_check` (Director tabtargets call `rbw-rdc` preflight before their cloud op)

Once those land, the paces look like:

**Pace α — RBRD regime + spec + handbook + render/validate.** Standard regime archetype, parallel to `rbrr_*`. New `rbrd_regime.sh`, `rbrd_cli.sh` with just `rbrd_validate`/`rbrd_render` (heavy ops follow in β), `RBSRD-RegimeDepot.adoc`, RBS0 index, `rbw-rdr`/`rbw-rdv` tabtargets, lifecycle marshal blanks, README updates, variable renames across `rbgp_Payor.sh`/`rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh`, `RBSDE` rename pass, handbook surface, `rbgc_Constants.sh` comment refresh. Names right; no enforcement.

**Pace β — Tripwire wiring: `rbrd_inscribe` + `rbrd_check` + preflight orchestration.** Add `rbrd_inscribe` and `rbrd_check` to `rbrd_cli.sh` (differential furnish for heavy deps). Add tabtargets `rbw-rdc`/`rbw-rdi`. Wire `rbw-dL` to invoke `rbw-rdi` at end of successful levy. Wire Director tabtargets that submit cloud ops (`rbw-fO`, `rbw-fA`, `rbw-fV`, etc.) to invoke `rbw-rdc` as preflight. `RBSDE` updated for levy-end inscribe step; identify and update the spec covering the preflight pattern; handbook updates for failure-mode + recovery (relevy or revert).

Sound right?

message.content:
re Q3) does this basically translate to having 2 variants of the checker function, one for each role?  Or does it naturally fall out that our _cli.sh _check function will use whatever RBRA is kindled, so its easy?  Maybe you grasp this already, I see in your Q4 context.

re Q4) Whoa, I think you do not yet understand BCG modules.  This should be very like other uses: a source, a kindle, call function; no layered tabtargets for this.  State clearly your intent after you consider this answer to prove you understand our current patterns.

re Q5) I think you don't have any more questions here, let me know if I missed it.

I'm good with 2 pace plan.  Ask any follow up questions, or proceed to slating the new paces; we'll call our current pace a 'planning pace only' I think, if that makes sense to you.

content.text:
**Q3 — Confirmed: one role-agnostic function.** `rbrd_check` takes the bearer token as a parameter (or sources whatever RBRA is already kindled in the calling CLI). The function itself doesn't care who you are — it just needs a GAR-read-capable token to do `docker login + pull + create + cp + rm + diff`. Same function called from Director-context CLIs (with Director token) and Retriever-context CLIs (with Retriever token). No variants. Each tabtarget remains single-SA, naturally.

**Q4 — Stating my corrected intent:**

I was wrong about tabtarget-level orchestration. The BCG pattern is direct module composition:

1. `rbrd_check` and `rbrd_inscribe` are **public functions** living in an implementation module — not in `rbrd_regime.sh` (which stays uniform with sibling regimes) and not synthesized via tabtarget layers.

2. A consuming CLI's `furnish` does the standard BCG dance: source the implementation module, kindle it, and the consuming CLI's command function calls `rbrd_check` directly as a regular function call.

3. Each consuming CLI's furnish — using differential furnish per command — sources the RBRD bespoke module only when the command in flight needs it (cloud-submitting commands need it; light commands don't).

4. There is no preflight tabtarget. No `rbw-rdc.sh` wrapping a `rbw-fO.sh`. The Director ordain command function itself calls `rbrd_check "${z_token}"` after kindle, before submitting the cloud build. If it dies, the cloud op never starts.

5. `rbrd_inscribe` likewise: Payor's levy command function calls it inline at the end of the successful levy path, in the same process. No tabtarget chaining.

The general shape: same as how any existing module's public function is called by another CLI — source, kindle, call. The check function is no different from `rbgi_add_*_iam` or `rbgu_get_governor_token_capture` in that regard.

**Q5 — Acknowledged, nothing more.**

---

**One follow-up before slating:**

Given the BCG decree that `rbrd_regime.sh` must stay uniform with sibling regimes, and given external CLIs need to source-and-kindle the bespoke functions, my read is the bespoke functions should live in **a separate implementation module** — call it `rbrdb_bespoke.sh` (or whatever name fits the prefix scheme; `rbrdb` is just a placeholder). Then:

- `rbrd_regime.sh` — pure regime archetype
- `rbrdb_bespoke.sh` — `rbrd_check`, `rbrd_inscribe` public functions (with own `zrbrdb_kindle` for docker-related constants and temp paths)
- `rbrd_cli.sh` — furnish sources both; dispatches `rbrd_render`, `rbrd_validate` (regime-only), `rbrd_check`, `rbrd_inscribe` (bespoke)
- Consuming CLIs (Director, Retriever, Payor-levy) — furnish sources `rbrdb_bespoke.sh` directly, kindles it, calls function inline

This puts the bespoke logic in an implementation module per BCG (so multiple CLI consumers can compose it cleanly via the standard pattern). `rbrd_cli.sh` exposes it via dispatch for tabtarget access.

**Is that the right structure, or did your "rbrd_cli.sh is appropriate for bespoke processing" mean you literally want the function bodies in the CLI file itself (and consuming CLIs source `rbrd_cli.sh` in their furnish)?**

Once I have that final architectural call, I can write the gazette for both paces and slate them. Calling ₢BOAAB a planning-only pace makes sense — the deliverable is the design captured here, the implementation goes to two new paces enrolled into ₣BO.

# jjezs_slate rbrd-regime-split

## Character

Intricate but mechanical. Sibling-regime archetype is well-established.

## Goal

Mint a new RBRD regime, peeling the four depot-time-immutable settings
out of RBRR. After this pace, names communicate the lifecycle category;
enforcement is the next pace.

## Locked decisions

- Four settings move: RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX, RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER,
  RBRR_GCP_REGION, RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE → RBRD_*. The remaining eight
  RBRR settings stay unchanged in RBRR.
- rbrd_regime.sh follows the sibling regime archetype uniformly — no
  bespoke code. New RBSRD-RegimeDepot.adoc parallels RBSRP and RBSRR.
- Lifecycle marshal (rblm_cli.sh) emits an rbrd.env blank alongside the
  rbrr.env blank.
- rbgc_Constants.sh's rbi_df comment block carries a stale "future RBRD
  in rbi_df" promise that becomes wrong with the next pace's decision
  (RBRD ships as its own image at a separate tag). Refresh the comment
  to reflect actual shape.
- No tripwire wiring in this pace. After this pace, names are right but
  a hand-edit of rbrd.env post-levy is still a silent no-op.

## Done

- rbw-rdr / rbw-rdv operate cleanly against a fresh rbrd.env blank.
- The four moved variables are absent from rbrr.env, present in rbrd.env.
- All consumers (rbgp_Payor.sh, rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh, anywhere else
  they are referenced) use the RBRD_* names.
- RBSDE rename pass complete; RBS0 index has the RBSRD entry.
- README regime list, file tree, and appendix entry mention RBRD.
- Handbook surfaces the new regime category.

# jjezs_slate rbrd-tripwire-wire

## Character

Intricate but mechanical. Established host-docker and image-extract
patterns throughout.

## Goal

Make depot-immutable drift fail loud. Levy inscribes a FROM-scratch
image carrying rbrd.env to GAR; every cloud-submitting command pulls
and exact-match diffs the file before proceeding.

## Locked decisions

- rbrd_check and rbrd_inscribe are public functions in a separate
  bespoke implementation module — not in rbrd_regime.sh (which must
  stay uniform with sibling regimes) and not in rbrd_cli.sh's body.
  rbrd_cli.sh sources the bespoke module and exposes both via dispatch
  with differential furnish. New tabtargets rbw-rdc and rbw-rdi.
- Consuming CLIs source the bespoke module, kindle it, call rbrd_check
  inline before cloud submission. No tabtarget preflight orchestration.
- rbrd_check takes a bearer token parameter; role-agnostic. Each
  consuming CLI passes its role's token. No new IAM grants.
- rbrd_inscribe runs host-side at end of successful levy using Payor's
  OAuth access token. Cloud Build is not involved.
- Pre-push existence guard via docker manifest inspect: present → fatal
  "already inscribed, unmake and relevy"; absent → proceed.
- Exact byte-match diff on rbrd.env contents — comments, whitespace,
  line ordering all participate.
- Image lives under rbi_df namespace; specific tag is a mount-time call.

## Done

- Fresh levy ends with the rbrd.env image present in GAR.
- Re-levy on an already-inscribed depot dies with overwrite-fatal.
- Every cloud-submitting command pulls + diffs before submitting; drift
  produces a diff + recovery guidance + fatal; image absent → fatal.
- RBSDE updated for the inscribe step at end of levy; the spec covering
  the kindle-time check identified and updated.
- Handbook documents drift, missing image, and re-inscribe overwrite
  failure modes with recovery guidance.

Both paces slated:

- **₢BOAAI** — `rbrd-regime-split` (regime mint, spec/handbook scaffolding, no enforcement yet)
- **₢BOAAJ** — `rbrd-tripwire-wire` (bespoke check/inscribe module, host-side inscribe at levy, inline kindle-time diff before cloud submissions)

Ordered after ₢BOAAB so the design conversation flows naturally into the implementation work.

**Ready to wrap ₢BOAAB as a planning-only pace?** If yes, I'll close it with a summary that captures the design decisions reached (regime split into RBRD, FROM-scratch image in `rbi_df` instead of bucket/ORAS/OCI primitives, docker host-side inscribe at levy, inline kindle-time check before cloud submissions, bespoke module architecture, exact byte-match comparison). Design conversation settled the depot-regime shape: four depot-time settings (RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX, RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER, RBRR_GCP_REGION, RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE) peel into a new RBRD regime; tripwire mechanism is a FROM-scratch image in rbi_df carrying rbrd.env, with host-side docker inscribe at Payor levy and host-side docker pull/extract/exact-byte-diff before every cloud submission. rbrd_check is role-agnostic taking a bearer token; consuming CLI furnishes source the bespoke module, kindle, call inline. Alternatives considered and rejected: ORAS host binary (violates narrow-deps), per-variable GCS bucket files (new IAM, new read mechanism, new write mechanism), per-variable GAR fact files (overcomplicated vs single image), Cloud-Build inscribe (needlessly complex for a small file). Comparison is exact byte-match rather than value-level so comment/whitespace edits also participate — sharper diagnostic than per-variable compare. Implementation slated as ₢BOAAI (rbrd-regime-split, regime mint + spec + handbook + variable rename) and ₢BOAAJ (rbrd-tripwire-wire, bespoke module + Payor host-side inscribe + role-agnostic check + inline call wiring at cloud-submission sites) following this pace."}

content.text:
₢BOAAB wrapped. Now the docket review. Verified two assumptions while reviewing — surfacing what I found.

## Review of ₢BOAAI (rbrd-regime-split)

**1. Migration of existing rbrr.env is undefined — likely a first-mount surprise.**
Operators with already-levied depots have `rbrr.env` files containing the four soon-to-be-moved variables. After α, sourcing rbrr.env loads four variables that are no longer declared in the new RBRR regime; per BCG regime archetype, `buv_scope_sentinel` catches undeclared `RBRR_*` and dies. The mount agent will hit this on their own rbrr.env (current canest depot included). Suggest adding to the locked decisions:
> Existing rbrr.env files require hand-migration: remove the four variables from rbrr.env, render a fresh rbrd.env via rbw-rdr (or lifecycle marshal blank), populate with the moved values. Document this in the handbook surface. No automatic migration code.

**2. "RBSDE rename pass" understates the spec surface.**
Several specs likely reference these variable names: RBSDE, RBSCO, RBSDN, RBSDU, the cloud-build specs (RBSCB, RBSCJ, RBSCTD), and possibly RBSRR itself. Replace "RBSDE rename pass complete" with a discovery recipe in the docket:
> Grep `RBRR_(CLOUD_PREFIX|DEPOT_MONIKER|GCP_REGION|GCB_MACHINE_TYPE)` across `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/`; update every reference site to RBRD_*. Removes the implied "only RBSDE matters" trap.

**3. Lifecycle marshal mechanism unverified.**
I asserted `rblm_cli.sh` emits the blank. Mount agent should verify the function shape (template emission vs in-place edit) matches before adding the RBRD path. Not a docket issue — just worth a note that this is a "follow the existing rbrr pattern" rather than novel work.

---

## Review of ₢BOAAJ (rbrd-tripwire-wire)

**4. Lock the bespoke module filename.**
Β's docket says "separate bespoke implementation module" without naming it. Suggest **`rbrdb_base.sh`** — follows the existing pattern (`rbhob_base.sh`, `rbhpb_base.sh`, `rbhwb_base.sh` are all "shared helpers" base modules). The `b` suffix is the established convention for this role. Without the lock in β's docket, mount may choose differently than what other files reference.

**5. Lock the exact image tag.**
"rbi_df namespace; specific tag is a mount-time call" creates writer/reader coordination risk inside one mount. Both `rbrd_inscribe` and `rbrd_check` need to agree on the exact ref. Suggest locking: **`rbi_df/rbrd:depot`** — singular meaning, parallels the probe's `rbi_df/probe-<variant>:probe` shape.

**6. Payor host-side `docker login` to GAR is a new path — flag for verification.**
Confirmed: `rbgp_Payor.sh` authenticates via `zrbgp_authenticate_capture` (OAuth → access token) but **never invokes `docker login`** today. Pouch/Pouches and all existing GAR pushes use Director/Retriever creds, not Payor. The OAuth access token *should* work with `docker login -u oauth2accesstoken --password-stdin` against the depot's GAR repo (Payor created the project), but this exact path is unexercised. Suggest adding to β's docket:
> Mount-time verification: confirm `docker login -u oauth2accesstoken --password-stdin <gar-host>` succeeds with a fresh Payor access token before building out `rbrd_inscribe`. If it doesn't, fallback decisions reopen.

**7. "Every cloud-submitting command" needs a discovery recipe, not vibes.**
Known Director cloud-submitting tabtargets in the current tree: `rbw-dE`, `rbw-dI`, `rbw-fA`, `rbw-fO`, `rbw-fV`. May also apply to Retriever's `rbw-fpf` (About fetch). Suggest replacing the vague phrasing with:
> Discovery recipe: grep `submit_build\|cloudbuild` across `Tools/rbk/*.sh`; identify consuming CLIs; each consuming CLI's furnish sources `rbrdb_base.sh`, kindles it, and the command function calls `rbrd_check` before submission.

**8. Pin the user-facing recovery message for drift-fatal.**
Overwrite-fatal text is locked ("already inscribed, unmake and relevy"). Drift-fatal isn't. Suggest pinning:
> On drift: emit the unified diff, then fatal: "RBRD has drifted from levied state. Either revert rbrd.env to the levied form, or `rbw-dU` then `rbw-dL` to relevy with the new values."

**9. Byte-faithful copy / no EOL normalization.**
Probable mount-agent temptation: "diff says the files differ by a trailing newline, let me normalize." Pre-empt:
> Locked decision: byte-for-byte preservation through `cp` into build context, `docker cp` out of pulled image, `diff` against operator's rbrd.env. No trimming, no whitespace normalization, no line-ending coercion. A trailing-newline difference IS drift.

---

## Recommended action

Want me to redocket both via `jjx_redocket` with these additions folded in? Suggestions 1, 2 land in α; 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 land in β. I'll keep the body length growth bounded — each addition is one or two locked-decision bullets.

lastPrompt: yes!  Then would you review both pace dockets and make a list of suggestions to reduce ambiguity and/ or errors in implementation?  I want this to work first time.

message.content:
Hmm, some thoughts:
1) There are zero backward compatibility concerns.  On electing this choice, I'll destroy all existing depots.  That removes a whole lot of complexity (and is a not-incidental reason we are doing this now)
2) Discovery recipe a great idea
3) Decide clearly whether you recommend a pace docket adjustment or whether you trust future you to come to the right conclusion without a change.
4) rbrdb_ violates minting due to collsion with rbrd_: do you understand that?  Didn't we adopt somewhere else a convention of rbnrRX_ as a prefix for regime 'R' where X can be custom?  See if you can locate that convention specification.  I think we may need to assure that is clearly stated in CLAUDE.md, buk*.md, and/ or BCG.
5) similar: use rbi_df/rbndf_fact:depot to have a clean unambiguous minting.  Or raise concerns if you have them. 
6) Interesting question.  Can you research and/ or experiment with current depot noninvasively to attempt clarification before we reslate?
7) reasonable
8) as long as you use buc_tabtarget and colophon references that are zipper variables, I'm happy.  Do tell me if you don't understand this
9) yes, byte-for-byte preservation.

Any more questions or issues before reslate?

125	# RBTOE: Payor OAuth Authentication Pattern
126	# Establishes Payor OAuth context by loading RBRO credentials and obtaining access token.
127	# Tokens are deliberately not cached — each call refreshes. Rationale: simplicity
128	# and freshness; refresh tokens are long-lived so the extra roundtrips are cheap
129	# relative to the depot operations they authorize, and uncached tokens can't grow
130	# stale between distinct Payor ceremonies.
131	zrbgp_authenticate_capture() {
132	  zrbgp_sentinel
133	  
134	  buc_log_args "Establishing Payor OAuth authentication context"
135	  
136	  # Load RBRO credentials
137	  rbgu_rbro_load
138	  
139	  # Load RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID from environment
140	  test -n "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID:-}" || buc_die "RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID not set in environment"
141	  
142	  # Exchange refresh token for access token
143	  local z_access_token
144	  z_access_token=$(zrbgp_refresh_capture) || buc_die "Failed to exchange OAuth refresh token"
145	  
146	  test -n "${z_access_token}" || buc_die "Empty access token from OAuth exchange"
147	  
148	  buc_log_args "Payor OAuth authentication successful"
149	  echo "${z_access_token}"
150	}

152	# Probe per-installation depot states and emit per-moniker fact files.
153	# Uses CRM v3 projects:search with displayName anchor as discriminator —
154	# query "displayName:RBGC-DEPOT*" returns ACTIVE and DELETE_REQUESTED
155	# projects together (no billing-account enumeration required).  Moniker is
156	# derived by stripping the "${RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX} " prefix from each
157	# project's displayName.  Fact files are namespaced by cloud_prefix to keep
158	# same-moniker depots under different cloud_prefixes from colliding:
159	# "<cloud_prefix>/<moniker>.${RBCC_fact_ext_depot}" (state) and
160	# "<cloud_prefix>/<moniker>.${RBCC_fact_ext_depot_project}" (project_id) are
161	# written via buf_write_fact_multi; the cloud_prefix subdir is derived from
162	# projectId (everything before the "-d-" infix). Consumers walking by
163	# moniker alone restrict to their own subdir; consumers auditing across all
164	# depots walk all subdirs.
165	#
166	# No RBRP_BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID required for enumeration; no Mason SA IAM scan.
167	# The displayName anchor is the sole discriminator.  Projects whose
168	# displayName does not parse to a valid RBRR-canonical moniker are skipped.
169	#
170	# Paginated via nextPageToken; per-page infixes keep response files distinct.
171	#
172	# Single-shot per dispatch — buf_write_fact_multi dies on preexist.

file.content:
echo "${z_access_token}"
}

# RBTOE: Payor OAuth Authentication Pattern
# Establishes Payor OAuth context by loading RBRO credentials and obtaining access token.
# Tokens are deliberately not cached — each call refreshes. Rationale: simplicity
# and freshness; refresh tokens are long-lived so the extra roundtrips are cheap
# relative to the depot operations they authorize, and uncached tokens can't grow
# stale between distinct Payor ceremonies.
zrbgp_authenticate_capture() {
  zrbgp_sentinel
  
  buc_log_args "Establishing Payor OAuth authentication context"
  
  # Load RBRO credentials
  rbgu_rbro_load
  
  # Load RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID from environment
  test -n "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID:-}" || buc_die "RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID not set in environment"
  
  # Exchange refresh token for access token
  local z_access_token
  z_access_token=$(zrbgp_refresh_capture) || buc_die "Failed to exchange OAuth refresh token"
  
  test -n "${z_access_token}" || buc_die "Empty access token from OAuth exchange"
  
  buc_log_args "Payor OAuth authentication successful"
  echo "${z_access_token}"
}

# Probe per-installation depot states and emit per-moniker fact files.
# Uses CRM v3 projects:search with displayName anchor as discriminator —
# query "displayName:RBGC-DEPOT*" returns ACTIVE and DELETE_REQUESTED
# projects together (no billing-account enumeration required).  Moniker is
# derived by stripping the "${RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX} " prefix from each
# project's displayName.  Fact files are namespaced by cloud_prefix to keep
# same-moniker depots under different cloud_prefixes from colliding:
# "<cloud_prefix>/<moniker>.${RBCC_fact_ext_depot}" (state) and
# "<cloud_prefix>/<moniker>.${RBCC_fact_ext_depot_project}" (project_id) are
# written via buf_write_fact_multi; the cloud_prefix subdir is derived from
# projectId (everything before the "-d-" infix). Consumers walking by
# moniker alone restrict to their own subdir; consumers auditing across all
# depots walk all subdirs.
#
# No RBRP_BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID required for enumeration; no Mason SA IAM scan.
# The displayName anchor is the sole discriminator.  Projects whose
# displayName does not parse to a valid RBRR-canonical moniker are skipped.
#
# Paginated via nextPageToken; per-page infixes keep response files distinct.
#
# Single-shot per dispatch — buf_write_fact_multi dies on preexist.
#
# Args: <oauth_access_token>
zrbgp_depot_state_emit() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  local -r z_token="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_token}" || buc_die "zrbgp_depot_state_emit: token required"

  local -r z_search_query="displayName:${RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX}*"
  local -r z_search_url_base="${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V3}/projects:search"

  # Anchor prefix string used inside loop for displayName stripping.
  local -r z_display_prefix="${RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX} "

  # Per-iteration synthesized locals (BCG exception 2 — declare outside, assign inside)
  local z_search_page=1
  local z_search_page_token=""
  local z_search_url=""
  local z_query_enc=""
  local z_tok_enc=""
  local z_search_infix=""
  local z_project_count=0
  local z_index=0
  local z_project_id=""

content.text:
Each point, with my decision (docket-change or trust-mount):

**1) Migration:** Dropped. Removing complexity is the win. No docket addition needed; rbrr.env in this clone can stay broken until you destroy depots.

**2) Discovery recipes:** Adopting for both #2 (RBSDE→broader spec sweep) and #7 (cloud-submitting commands). **Docket change to both α and β.**

**3) Decisive recommendations follow.** Will state docket-change or trust-mount per item.

**4) Minting violation — yes, I understand and you're right.** `rbrd_` names the regime (terminal); `rbrdb_` extends it as if `rbrd` had children. Forbidden by Rule 2.

**Search for the convention you mentioned:** I grepped `Tools/rbk` for `rbnr*` files and the term in `vov_veiled/` specs — **zero hits**. The convention is not currently written down anywhere. The `rbho/rbhob` precedent I cited earlier is a different shape (rbho is non-terminal handbook-onboarding family with many children); it's not the regime-extension convention you're describing.

**Proposed shape, parsing your "rbnrRX_":**
- `rbn` — non-terminal (today holds `rbnnh_` nameplate-customization)
- `rbnr` — non-terminal, new: "regime extension" family
- `rbnrd` — terminal: bespoke RBRD (D=depot regime selector)
- File: `rbnrd_base.sh` (single helper file; both `rbnrd_check` and `rbnrd_inscribe` as public functions)
- If we ever do RBRA/RBRN/etc extensions: `rbnra_`, `rbnrn_`, etc.

Functions become `rbnrd_check` / `rbnrd_inscribe` (not `rbrd_*` — file prefix = function prefix per BCG). Tabtargets `rbw-rdc` / `rbw-rdi` still good (the colophon doesn't have to echo the function name; zipper handles the binding).

**Doc question for you:** The `rbnr*` convention isn't documented anywhere. Two paths:
- **(a)** Fold a small "regime extension prefix family" mint into α's docket so CLAUDE.md / minting study / a BCG addendum learns it before β depends on it
- **(b)** Document it as part of β when we actually create `rbnrd_base.sh` (lighter touch — convention introduced by usage with one paragraph in the relevant header)

I'd lean (a) — α is already doing minting and doc work; β stays implementation-only. **What's your call?**

**5) `rbi_df/rbndf_fact:depot` — clean, no concerns.** Decoding for the record:
- `rbn` non-terminal → `rbnd` non-terminal (depot-fact family) → `rbndf` terminal (the GAR image package name)
- Tag `:depot` (singular, locks meaning)
- No terminal-exclusivity collision with anything currently in tree

**Docket change to β:** lock image ref as `<gar-host>/<project>/<repo>/rbi_df/rbndf_fact:depot`.

**6) Payor docker auth verification, noninvasive research:**

What I confirmed without modifying anything:
- `zrbgp_authenticate_capture` (rbgp_Payor.sh:131) returns a vanilla OAuth user-account access_token via the standard `client_id + refresh_token` → access_token exchange (line 144 calls `zrbgp_refresh_capture`)
- This token is what Payor uses for all subsequent REST calls including `artifactregistry.googleapis.com` (project/repo creation, IAM management, lien ops — line 1046 grants `roles/artifactregistry.writer` to the Mason SA using this same token via REST API)
- GAR's docker registry endpoint `<region>-docker.pkg.dev` accepts OAuth user account tokens via `docker login -u oauth2accesstoken -p <token>` — this is the documented Google pattern (and what `gcloud auth configure-docker` does internally)
- Payor's OAuth identity holds Owner on the project they created; Owner includes `artifactregistry.writer`

**Conclusion:** Payor → `docker login` → GAR push should work. Risk is low. Mount-time smoke test is ~30 seconds: get a Payor token via a quick rbgv probe, try `docker login -u oauth2accesstoken --password-stdin` against `<region>-docker.pkg.dev`, observe the exit code. **No docket change needed**; if mount-time smoke fails, the documented fallback is Cloud Build push (rejected earlier, but available).

I did NOT try a live experiment because acquiring a fresh Payor token without writing new code requires running an existing Payor command and snooping — there's no honest way to do that noninvasively given BCG's "tokens never touch disk" discipline. The research path is solid enough that I'm comfortable not docketing the verification step.

**7) Cloud-submitting discovery recipe:** Adopting. **Docket change to β** with the known-Director-set as a hint + grep recipe.

**8) Zipper variables — yes, I understand.** Zipper-registered colophon constants are the reference path in code; hardcoded `"rbw-rdc"` strings stay out of `.sh` files. Tabtargets themselves are the wire-format colophons; in-code references use the zipper constants (e.g., something like `RBWZ_COLOPHON_RBRD_CHECK` or whatever the naming convention surfaces). The docket can still NAME the colophons (`rbw-rdc`/`rbw-rdi`) since dockets are spec text, not code. **No docket change**, but I'll note "use zipper-registered colophon constants in code, not hardcoded strings" as part of the locked-decisions block in β.

**9) Byte-for-byte:** Adopting. **Docket change to β.**

---

**Summary of docket changes planned:**

α changes:
- Add discovery recipe for RBS*.adoc rename sweep (replaces vague "RBSDE rename pass")
- (Pending your answer) optionally: fold rbnr* convention documentation work into α

β changes:
- Lock bespoke module: `rbnrd_base.sh` with `rbnrd_check` and `rbnrd_inscribe`
- Lock image ref: `rbi_df/rbndf_fact:depot`
- Discovery recipe for cloud-submitting commands (with known Director set as hint)
- Drift-fatal message text pinned
- Byte-for-byte preservation note (no normalization)
- Zipper-constant requirement for in-code colophon references

**One pending question:** doc home for the `rbnr*` minting convention — (a) folded into α or (b) introduced inline by β. Otherwise ready to reslate.

content: We're slating two paces in heat BO for the depot-immutable regime work; both dockets are ready to redocket with the changes we agreed on. Next: you decide whether the new rbnr* minting convention gets documented in pace α or introduced inline by pace β. (disable recaps in /config)

content.text:
**Scout result: zero hits across all heats and paces.**

I searched the upcoming-pace corpus for: `rbnr`, `rbnnh`, `RBNNH`, `RBSCH`, `rbnd`, `rbnr_`, `charge_hook`, `post_charge`, `regime extension`, `regime helper`, `regime customization`, `extension prefix`, `custom prefix`, `rbn prefix`, `nameplate customization`, `regime tripwire`, `depot facts`. Nothing matches.

What DOES exist in the corpus around prefix discipline:
- **`bespoke`** appears only in our two just-slated paces (₢BOAAI, ₢BOAAJ) — no prior pace introduces this notion
- **`terminal exclusivity`** appears in 9 paces across multiple heats — but each invokes the *rule*, not the regime-extension *workaround*. Examples: `mint-rbj-jailer-prefix` (₢AUAAa) resolved a violation by adopting a new prefix; `calibrant-fixture-foundation` (₢BBAAh) picked classifier `l` because `c` was owned by crucible; `vof-prefix-tree-checker` (₢ADAAE rough) is the *future* checker that will enforce the rule mechanically
- **`minting`** appears in many paces but always as the rule, never as "the regime-extension convention"
- **`rbnnh_`** is documented in `CLAUDE.md` for nameplate-customization but nowhere in the pace corpus — and it's a different domain (per-nameplate hooks, not regime extensions)

**Conclusion:** The `rbnr{R}{X}_` regime-extension convention is genuinely unspecified. It hasn't been minted, hasn't been documented in any paddock or pace, and doesn't exist in code. Your recollection points at a pattern you intended but never recorded.

This sharpens my earlier question. The convention has to be *invented* somewhere before β can legitimately use `rbnrd_base.sh`. Three options:

**(a)** Mint and document `rbnr{R}_` in α (fold into the docket).
α already does spec/handbook work; adding a small "regime extension prefix family" mint to CLAUDE.md's prefix registry and the minting memo (`Memos/memo-20260110-acronym-selection-study.md`) is a low-cost addition. β then references it.

**(b)** Mint it inline in β with a `## Minting record` block at the top of `rbnrd_base.sh`'s header comment (precedent: `₢AUAAa mint-rbj-jailer-prefix` did this for the `rbj` prefix). Lighter touch, no separate doc updates; documentation arrives in β alongside first use.

**(c)** Slate a third pace ahead of β: a pure minting pace that adds `rbnr*` to CLAUDE.md and the minting memo. Cleanest separation but most ceremony for a one-paragraph addition.

Given the precedent of `₢AUAAa` (minting-record-in-header inside the pace that first uses the prefix), I'd recommend **(b)** — mint `rbnr{R}_` inline in β with a minting-record block, keeping α focused on its regime-archetype scope. The convention becomes documented by its first use.

**Your call — (a), (b), or (c)?**

Then I'll redocket and we're ready to roll.

content.text:
**Scout result for `rbnn`: zero hits in pace corpus.** Confirmed `rbnnh_` exists only in code as the per-nameplate hook convention (RBNNH in CLAUDE.md, files like `.rbk/tadmor/rbnnh_compose.yml`) — but that's user-authored files in per-instance directories, a different domain from "kit bespoke code attached to a regime."

**File structure reality I missed in my earlier analysis:**

Looking at `Tools/rbk/rbg*.sh` and `Tools/rbk/rbf*.sh`: every kit module is exactly 2 files — `<prefix>_cli.sh` + `<prefix>_<Name>.sh`. The rbr* regime modules are uniformly 2-file too. Adding `rbrd_base.sh` would make rbrd a 3-file module — non-uniform within the kit family.

More importantly: **`zrbrd_kindle()` would have to hold the bespoke constants too** (BCG requires all kindle constants in kindle), which contaminates `rbrd_regime.sh` with non-regime state and breaks the per-file uniformity you explicitly required. So adding `rbrd_base.sh` in the same module isn't viable — we'd still violate uniformity, just in a different file.

This means we genuinely **do need a separate module with its own prefix and its own kindle**. Three real candidates:

**(B1) Mint `rbnr{R}_` family per your intuition**
- `rbn` non-terminal (existing, holds rbnnh)
- `rbnr` non-terminal NEW (regime extensions)
- `rbnrd` terminal (bespoke for RBRD)
- File: `rbnrd_base.sh` with `zrbnrd_kindle()`, public `rbnrd_check`, `rbnrd_inscribe`
- Future regime extensions: `rbnra_`, `rbnrn_`, etc.

**(B2) Mint `rbtw_` for the tripwire mechanism specifically**
- Independent prefix, no family invented
- Functions `rbtw_check`, `rbtw_inscribe` — domain-named (tripwire) not regime-named
- Single mint, no family commitment

**(B3) Different prefix entirely** — your call if you have a better letter in mind

**Mint-record-in-place pattern:** β includes the minting record in the new file's header (precedent: `₢AUAAa mint-rbj-jailer-prefix` did exactly this for the `rbj` prefix), plus a one-line addition to CLAUDE.md's Project Prefix Registry table. No separate doc-update pace needed.

**My lean:** **(B1) `rbnrd_base.sh`** — it matches your intuition, makes sense as a family ("regime extensions" might apply to other regimes someday — RBRR has 8 surviving variables, some of which may want similar treatment), and the minting record is self-contained.

If you prefer (B2) for "don't commit to a family until we need one," that's also defensible.

**Pick B1, B2, or propose B3, and I'll redocket and slate.**

content: Goal: slate two paces in ₣BO for the RBRD depot-regime work (regime split + tripwire). Awaiting your pick of bespoke-module prefix: B1 `rbnrd_base.sh` (mint `rbnr*` family), B2 `rbtw_base.sh` (mint tripwire prefix), or B3 your own choice. Once chosen, I redocket β and we're done.

message.content: Hold on you _did_ find the use of the convention: rbnnh_ is a 'nameplate' regime 'hook' concept, where first 'n' is just a free letter that is regime affiliated but allows minting.  Does it seem used that way?  It would change our minting to rbndf_ right?

content.text:
You're right — I was reading `rbnnh_` too narrowly. Decoding it your way:

- `rb` — project
- `n` — extension marker ("regime-affiliated namespace that allows minting under a terminal regime prefix")
- `n` — regime letter (nameplate)
- `h` — kind (hook)

Applied to the depot regime + fact kind: **`rbndf_`** ✓ (which is what we'd already locked for the image package: `rbi_df/rbndf_fact:depot`). Decoding matches perfectly — `rb + n + d (depot) + f (fact)`.

So the convention IS in code, I just hadn't decoded the letter positions. It already governs `rbnnh_` (existing) and `rbndf_` (the image we just minted). Two uses → it's a real pattern, not a one-off.

**What this means for the bespoke code module:**

Same shape: `rbnd{kind}_<name>.sh` where kind is the letter that captures the file's role. The bespoke RBRD module holds `rbnd?_check` + `rbnd?_inscribe`. Candidates for the kind letter:

- **`rbndb_base.sh`** — `b` for "base helpers" (matches the `_base.sh` precedent in `rbhob_base.sh`, `rbhpb_base.sh`, `rbhwb_base.sh`)
- **`rbndt_tripwire.sh`** — `t` for "tripwire" (names the operational mechanism)
- **`rbndi_image.sh`** — `i` for "image" (both functions operate on the depot regime image)

I'd lean **`rbndb_base.sh`** — `b` for base mirrors the rbhob/rbhpb/rbhwb precedent for "shared helper module under a parent group," and avoids overcommitting the kind letter to a specific operational metaphor.

**Confirm the decoding and pick a kind letter, then I'll redocket β with:**
- Module file: `rbnd{your-pick}_base.sh` (let's say `rbndb_base.sh`)
- Public functions: `rbndb_check`, `rbndb_inscribe`
- Image ref: `<gar>/<project>/<repo>/rbi_df/rbndf_fact:depot` (unchanged)
- Minting record in `rbndb_base.sh`'s header documenting the `rbn{regime}{kind}_` convention with one-line addition to CLAUDE.md's prefix registry

Sound right?

input.content:
# jjezs_reslate ₢BOAAI

## Character

Intricate but mechanical. Sibling-regime archetype is well-established.

## Goal

Mint a new RBRD regime, peeling the four depot-time-immutable settings
out of RBRR. After this pace, names communicate the lifecycle category;
enforcement is the next pace.

## Locked decisions

- Four settings move: RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX, RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER,
  RBRR_GCP_REGION, RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE → RBRD_*. The remaining eight
  RBRR settings stay unchanged in RBRR.
- rbrd_regime.sh follows the sibling regime archetype uniformly — no
  bespoke code. New RBSRD-RegimeDepot.adoc parallels RBSRP and RBSRR.
- Lifecycle marshal (rblm_cli.sh) emits an rbrd.env blank alongside the
  rbrr.env blank.
- rbgc_Constants.sh's rbi_df comment block carries a stale "future RBRD
  in rbi_df" promise that becomes wrong with the next pace (RBRD ships
  as a single image at rbi_df/rbndf_fact:depot, not per-variable files).
  Refresh the comment to reflect actual shape.
- Zero backward compatibility: existing depots will be destroyed and
  relevied. No migration code, no fallback paths.
- No tripwire wiring in this pace. After this pace, names are right but
  a hand-edit of rbrd.env post-levy is still a silent no-op.

## Done

- rbw-rdr / rbw-rdv operate cleanly against a fresh rbrd.env blank.
- The four moved variables are absent from rbrr.env, present in rbrd.env.
- All code consumers use RBRD_* — discovery recipe: grep
  `RBRR_\(CLOUD_PREFIX\|DEPOT_MONIKER\|GCP_REGION\|GCB_MACHINE_TYPE\)`
  across Tools/rbk/*.sh and tt/ and update every reference site.
- Spec rename pass — discovery recipe: same grep across
  Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/ and update every reference site. RBS0 index
  has the RBSRD entry.
- README regime list, file tree, and appendix entry mention RBRD.
- Handbook surfaces the new regime category.

# jjezs_reslate ₢BOAAJ

## Character

Intricate but mechanical. Established host-docker and image-extract
patterns throughout.

## Goal

Make depot-immutable drift fail loud. Levy inscribes a FROM-scratch
image carrying rbrd.env to GAR; every cloud-submitting command pulls
and exact-match diffs before proceeding.

## Locked decisions

- New module rbndb_base.sh + rbndb_cli.sh. Public functions:
  rbndb_check, rbndb_inscribe. rbndb_ minted under the rbn{R}{X}_
  convention (n=extension marker, d=depot regime, b=base helpers);
  self-contained mint record in rbndb_base.sh header. Follow-on pace
  lifts the convention doc to its canonical project home.
- Consuming CLIs source rbndb_base.sh, kindle zrbndb_kindle, call
  rbndb_check inline before cloud submission. No tabtarget preflight
  orchestration.
- rbndb_check: takes bearer token parameter; role-agnostic. No new
  IAM grants — every consuming role already has GAR read.
- rbndb_inscribe: host-side at end of successful levy using Payor
  OAuth access token. No Cloud Build. Pre-push existence guard via
  docker manifest inspect: present → fatal "RBRD already inscribed —
  unmake and relevy"; absent (404) → proceed.
- Image ref: <gar-host>/<project>/<repo>/rbi_df/rbndf_fact:depot.
  rbndf_ minted under same rbn{R}{X}_ convention, kind f=fact.
- Exact byte-match diff on rbrd.env — comments, whitespace, line
  ordering, trailing newlines all participate. No normalization.
- Drift fatal: emit unified diff, then fatal with text matching
  "RBRD has drifted from levied state. Either revert rbrd.env to
  the levied form, or `rbw-dU` then `rbw-dL` to relevy with the
  new values."
- Cloud-submission discovery recipe: grep `cloudbuild\|submit_build`
  across Tools/rbk/*.sh; wire rbndb_check into each consuming CLI's
  furnish and command function.
- In-code colophon references use zipper-registered constants, never
  hardcoded strings. Tabtargets rbw-rdc and rbw-rdi.

## Done

- Fresh levy ends with image present at the locked ref.
- Re-levy on an already-inscribed depot dies with overwrite-fatal.
- Every cloud-submitting command pulls + diffs; drift produces unified
  diff + recovery guidance + fatal; image absent → fatal.
- RBSDE updated for the inscribe step at end of levy; the spec
  covering the kindle-time check identified and updated.
- Handbook documents drift, missing image, and overwrite failure
  modes with recovery guidance.
- rbndb_base.sh header carries self-contained rbn{R}{X}_ mint record.

input.content:
# jjezs_slate rbnRX-convention-document

## Character

Design conversation requiring judgment. The convention is already in
use by code (rbnnh_ for nameplate hooks, rbndf_ for depot facts, rbndb_
for depot regime bespoke helpers); this pace decides where its formal
documentation lives and writes it there.

## Goal

Lift the self-contained mint record from rbndb_base.sh into the
canonical project documentation location for the rbn{R}{X}_ convention.

## Locked decisions

- Convention: `rbn{regime-letter}{kind-letter}_` is a regime-affiliated
  namespace that allows minting under a terminal regime prefix. Decode:
  `rb` (project) + `n` (extension marker, sidesteps terminal exclusivity
  on regime prefixes) + regime letter + kind letter. Existing uses to
  enumerate: rbnnh_ (nameplate hook), rbndf_ (depot fact image),
  rbndb_ (depot regime bespoke helpers).

## What to decide at mount

- Canonical doc home — candidates: CLAUDE.md prefix registry section,
  a buk*.md guide, BCG addendum, the acronym-selection minting study
  memo (Memos/memo-20260110-...), or a new dedicated location.
  Decide based on the convention's character: project-level naming
  rule (CLAUDE.md fits), BUK-pattern (buk*.md fits), or BCG-internal
  discipline (BCG fits).
- Cross-references: regardless of canonical home, CLAUDE.md's Project
  Prefix Registry or minting workflow should reference the convention
  so future minters discover it.

## Done

- Convention documented in the chosen location with letter-position
  decoding, existing uses enumerated, guidance for future application.
- rbndb_base.sh header trimmed to point at the canonical location
  rather than restating the convention.
- CLAUDE.md references the convention (at minimum a pointer; full
  prose may live elsewhere depending on the home decision).

use by code (rbnnh_ for nameplate hooks, rbndf_ for depot facts, rbndb_
for depot regime bespoke helpers); this pace decides where its formal
documentation lives and writes it there.

## Goal

Lift the self-contained mint record from rbndb_base.sh into the
canonical project documentation location for the rbn{R}{X}_ convention.

## Locked decisions

- Convention: `rbn{regime-letter}{kind-letter}_` is a regime-affiliated
  namespace that allows minting under a terminal regime prefix. Decode:
  `rb` (project) + `n` (extension marker, sidesteps terminal exclusivity
  on regime prefixes) + regime letter + kind letter. Existing uses to
  enumerate: rbnnh_ (nameplate hook), rbndf_ (depot fact image),
  rbndb_ (depot regime bespoke helpers).

## What to decide at mount

- Canonical doc home — candidates: CLAUDE.md prefix registry section,
  a buk*.md guide, BCG addendum, the acronym-selection minting study
  memo (Memos/memo-20260110-...), or a new dedicated location.
  Decide based on the convention's character: project-level naming
  rule (CLAUDE.md fits), BUK-pattern (buk*.md fits), or BCG-internal
  discipline (BCG fits).
- Cross-references: regardless of canonical home, CLAUDE.md's Project
  Prefix Registry or minting workflow should reference the convention
  so future minters discover it.

## Done

- Convention documented in the chosen location with letter-position
  decoding, existing uses enumerated, guidance for future application.
- rbndb_base.sh header trimmed to point at the canonical location
  rather than restating the convention.
- CLAUDE.md references the convention (at minimum a pointer; full
  prose may live elsewhere depending on the home decision).

content.text: ₢BOAAK

Three paces locked in:
- **₢BOAAI** rbrd-regime-split (redocketed)
- **₢BOAAJ** rbrd-tripwire-wire (redocketed)
- **₢BOAAK** rbnRX-convention-document (new)

---

## Loose ends from this chat — short list

**1. Scope check: "cloud submission" excludes reads.**
We agreed tripwire fires "before every cloud submission." Plumb / Tally / Summon / Wrest are READ-only (no `submit_build`); they bypass the tripwire by design. The β discovery recipe (`grep cloudbuild\|submit_build`) naturally honors this. Confirm if you intended any read ops to also trigger the check.

**2. This clone breaks the moment α lands.**
Your `.rbk/rbrr.env` here still has the four soon-to-move variables. After α merges, `rbw-rrv` on this clone will fail until you either hand-edit rbrr.env or destroy-and-relevy. Pace α's mount will hit this themselves — expected, but worth a one-line operational note in your head: don't merge α onto a workstation that's mid-debugging on canest.

**3. 96-core quota goes away with canest.**
Destroying canest (your intent under "zero backward compat") forfeits the 96-core e2-highcpu allocation we lobbied for. The follow-on relevy will be e2-standard-2 anyway (per ₢BOAAA's locked decision), so this aligns — but the quota itself is gone for good unless re-requested.

**4. ₢BOAAA still rough.**
That A/B bench pace from ₣BO is still ahead of these three in the heat but unaffected by the design we just did. Mount order is yours.

**5. Test fixture additions.**
- α: extend regime-validation fixture to cover RBRD (rblm/rbrn-style pattern).
- β: needs a tripwire-flavored fixture — possibly service tier (needs live depot for inscribe verification). Mount agent will discover the closest existing pattern.
Not blocking the slate; mount can decide fixture scope.

**6. rbw-rdi as user-callable is mildly weird.**
After levy, `rbw-rdi` always hits overwrite-fatal. Safe behavior but operator-confusing. Could de-scope as a tabtarget (keep the function callable only from the levy path). Minor; leave to mount.

**7. Payor host-side `docker login` to GAR is unexercised today.**
Research showed it should work (OAuth user token + Owner role + Google's standard auth). Mount-time smoke test (one `docker login` attempt with a fresh Payor token) is the gate before fully wiring `rbndb_inscribe`. Falls back to Cloud Build push (rejected as overcomplicated, but available) if smoke fails. Documented here, not in docket.

That's everything from the chat. Three paces ready to mount in order.
